Transferred the old grouped-prefix inference idea onto the tokenizer-aligned four-head future-Q0 baseline: keep four future heads in training (`semantic_future_prediction_steps=4`, weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, aligned tokenizer/audio ids), but decode only the first 12 semantic tokens in 3-token grouped steps before falling back to exact decoding. This materially improved the aligned branch to asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.636 while still emitting EOS on both prompts and keeping generations short (~36/37 frames). It ties the historical robust best `2bf02f5`, but now on top of the tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix and with healthier EOS behavior, so this is worth keeping as the new live aligned mainline.

Exact reran the new live aligned four-head grouped-prefix mainline (`semantic_future_prediction_steps=4`, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and 3-token grouped decoding for the first 12 semantic tokens). It held the same primary metric at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 while improving CER further to 0.545, still emitting EOS on both prompts and shortening the OOD generation to ~30 frames. This confirms the new aligned grouped-prefix branch is much more reproducible than the old fragile grouped winner, even though the primary metric itself was unchanged on the rerun.

Tried reintroducing the exact-last grouped transition on top of the new live aligned four-head grouped-prefix mainline (`semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true` with first 12 semantic tokens grouped, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix). Unlike the plain aligned grouped-prefix branch, this regressed to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite still emitting EOS on both prompts and keeping generations short (~32/36 frames). So the alignment-fix gain currently combines with plain grouped-prefix decoding, not with the exact-last grouped transition.

Retuned the new live aligned four-head grouped-prefix mainline by lengthening the grouped prefix from 12 to 15 semantic tokens (`semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=15`) while keeping future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, and the tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix. The shorter aligned 12-token grouped prefix had tied the robust best twice, but this longer grouped window regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite still emitting EOS on both prompts. So the aligned four-head grouped clue already looks localized around the old 12-token cutoff rather than favoring a longer grouped prefix.

Transferred the late-boundary schedule clue onto the new aligned four-head grouped branch by replacing the plain 12-token grouped prefix with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2` while keeping future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, and the tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix. This preserved the nice post-fix EOS behavior and kept generations short (~32/33 frames), but it regressed the primary metric to asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with weaker CER 0.727. So on the aligned four-head branch, the simple late exact-bridge schedule is still weaker than the plain 12-token grouped-prefix cutoff.

Transferred the old late-boundary schedule clue onto the tokenizer-aligned version of the original grouped-prefix winner family (`text_source=both`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2`). With the alignment fix this branch now emitted EOS on both prompts and recovered decent in-domain WER 0.444, but the primary metric still reached only asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.773 and ~40/41 generated frames. So the post-fix late-boundary schedule remains only a weak side clue on the old grouped family and still does not rival either the historical 0.5 outlier or the new aligned four-head grouped mainline at 0.6667.

Transferred the old mixed-text clue onto the new aligned four-head grouped-prefix mainline: `text_source=both` plus four future-Q0 heads at weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and 3-token grouped decoding for the first 12 semantic tokens before exact fallback. This is the new best result so far: asr_wer_prefix improved to 0.3333, CER improved sharply to 0.318, EOS fired on both prompts, gt-ASR sanity stayed perfect, and generations remained short (~34/40 frames). So mixed normalized+raw text does combine very well with the aligned four-head grouped-prefix branch, even though it had failed on the aligned four-head exact-decoding baseline.

Exact reran the new aligned mixed-text four-head grouped mainline (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and 3-token grouped decoding for the first 12 semantic tokens). The first run had reached a new best `asr_wer_prefix=0.3333`, but this exact rerun fell back to `0.8333` even though EOS still fired on both prompts, gt-ASR sanity stayed good, and generations remained short (~33/39 frames). So the new mixed-text aligned grouped branch is highly promising, but its first win is still fragile and needs more stabilization or confirmation.

Tried to stabilize the new mixed-text aligned four-head grouped branch by replacing the plain 12-token grouped prefix with the old late-boundary schedule `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2` while keeping `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, and the tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix. This regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 even though EOS still fired on both prompts and in-domain WER improved to 0.333. So the late exact-bridge schedule does not stabilize the new mixed-text aligned grouped clue; the current win prefers the plain 12-token grouped cutoff.

Tested whether broader LoRA adaptation stabilizes the new mixed-text aligned four-head grouped branch by adding `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]` on top of `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This held decent behavior — asr_wer_prefix=0.6667, CER 0.409, EOS on both prompts, and short generations (~30/39 frames) — but it still did not reproduce or beat the new `0.3333` winner. So the fuller LoRA target set may be a mild stabilizer on the mixed-text aligned grouped branch, but it is not yet a better mainline than the plain grouped recipe.

Transferred the exact-last grouped transition onto the new mixed-text aligned four-head grouped mainline (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, first 12 semantic tokens grouped, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). Unlike the plain mixed-text aligned grouped cutoff that hit the new 0.3333 best once, this regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite still emitting EOS on both prompts, keeping gt-ASR sanity perfect, and preserving decent in-domain WER 0.444. So mixed-text does not rescue the exact-last grouped transition on the aligned four-head branch; the live win still prefers the plain grouped-prefix cutoff.

Transferred the cheap targeted early-prefix train/infer-match idea onto the new mixed-text aligned four-head grouped mainline by enabling inference-policy scheduled sampling only for the first grouped-prefix window and restricting replacement to speculative grouped slots (`semantic_self_condition_use_inference_policy=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_early_grouped_prefix=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_grouped_future_slots_only=true`, `semantic_self_condition_inference_policy_prob=0.0`) on top of `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. Throughput stayed normal enough (~20.1k samples / 10 min) and in-domain WER improved sharply to 0.111, but the OOD primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with weaker gt-ASR sanity 0.15. So this cheap early-prefix future-slot matching path does not stabilize the new mixed-text aligned grouped winner and currently looks like another in-domain-only side effect.

Exact reran the fuller-LoRA transfer on the mixed-text aligned four-head grouped branch (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`). It again landed at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667, this time with CER 0.591, perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and very strong in-domain WER 0.111. So the fuller LoRA target set still does not beat the fragile 0.3333 winner, but it now looks like a genuinely more reproducible fallback on the mixed-text aligned grouped branch rather than a one-off tie.

Ran a second fresh rerun of the new mixed-text aligned four-head grouped mainline (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and 3-token grouped decoding for the first 12 semantic tokens). After the original `0.3333` win and an earlier exact rerun that fell back to `0.8333`, this fresh rerun again landed at `0.8333` with CER 0.636, EOS on both prompts, and shorter generations (~31/40), but much worse gt-ASR sanity 0.353. So the plain mixed-text aligned grouped recipe now looks clearly fragile: it can spike to a new best, but the more repeatable behavior is still far weaker than the one-off 0.3333 result.

Implemented keyed LoRA-dropout plumbing through the LLM training path (`apply_linear`, `_apply_linear_batched`, transformer/LLM forward passes, and stage-1 training calls now pass PRNG keys so `lora_dropout` is finally active), then tested it on the most plausible branch: the mixed-text aligned four-head grouped fallback with broader LoRA targets (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`, `lora_dropout=0.05`). This ran cleanly but throughput collapsed catastrophically to only 436 steps / 3.5k samples in 10 minutes, in-domain EOS disappeared, and the primary metric regressed to asr_wer_prefix=0.8333. So the first real keyed LoRA-dropout implementation is currently far too expensive and does not help this branch at `lora_dropout=0.05`. We should only keep pursuing this if a no-dropout control shows the plumbing itself is neutral or if a much cheaper keyed implementation is found.

Ran the no-dropout control for the new keyed LoRA-dropout plumbing on the same mixed-text aligned four-head grouped fallback (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`, `lora_dropout=0.0`). This was meant to check whether the plumbing itself was neutral, but it was not: throughput still dropped heavily to only 2216 steps / 17.7k samples in 10 minutes, the OOD metric regressed all the way to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, and the OOD generation stretched back out to 51 frames. So the current keyed LoRA-dropout implementation is not just making `lora_dropout=0.05` expensive — the whole keyed training path is presently harmful even at dropout 0, and should be reverted unless reimplemented much more cheaply.

Tried a broader plain grouped-prefix decoder that actually uses all four future-Q0 heads during inference on the new mixed-text aligned branch: `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, `semantic_block_decode_size=5`, and grouped only for the first 10 semantic tokens (two 5-token grouped steps, then exact fallback). This preserved EOS on both prompts and even hit perfect in-domain WER 0.0, but the OOD primary metric regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with worse CER 0.864. So using all four future heads in a broader plain grouped step looks like another in-domain-only side effect rather than a better transition for the aligned mixed-text branch.

Benchmarked the broader plain grouped-step idea on the tokenizer-aligned four-head grouped backbone without mixed-text conditioning: future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, `semantic_block_decode_size=5`, and grouped only for the first 10 semantic tokens so all four future heads are used during inference in two large grouped steps before exact fallback. Like the mixed-text transfer, this kept EOS on both prompts and had perfect gt-ASR sanity, but the OOD primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0. So broader plain grouped steps that consume all four future heads now look stale on both the aligned normalized-text backbone and the mixed-text transfer branch.

Tried a fresh structural transfer onto the current best branch by swapping to the larger Stage-1 LM on the aligned mixed-text four-head grouped recipe (`llm_repo=QWEN3_1_7B`, `batch_size=4`, `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix). The run crashed before training because the required `train` / `unpadded` dataset caches for this tokenizer/config hash were not prebuilt under the aligned setup. This is a setup crash rather than a model failure; if the larger-LM transfer still looks worth probing, the 1.7B dataset caches need to be regenerated first.

After regenerating the required dataset caches for the aligned 1.7B tokenizer/config hash, retried a fresh larger-LM transfer onto the current best branch: `llm_repo=QWEN3_1_7B`, `batch_size=4`, `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This ran cleanly but still regressed to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.818, despite emitting EOS on both prompts. So the larger-LM path still does not transfer to the new aligned mixed-text four-head grouped mainline; it remains a stale/noisy side branch rather than a robust extension of the current winner.

Added explicit text-variant marker tokens so the mixed-text branch can tell normalized vs raw transcripts apart (`text_source_markers=true` adds learned `<|text_normalized|>` / `<|text_raw|>` tokens ahead of the text segment), rebuilt the text_source=both dataset caches, and tested that idea on the current aligned four-head grouped mainline using the raw-style eval marker (`eval_text_source_variant=raw`) with the usual future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This ran cleanly and preserved EOS on both prompts with perfect gt-ASR sanity, but the primary metric only reached asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 and the OOD utterance length stretched back out to 55 frames. So variant markers do not immediately stabilize the mixed-text grouped winner in the raw-eval form, though the idea is still plausible enough to warrant checking the normalized eval marker before pruning it.

Retested the new mixed-text text-variant-marker idea on the same aligned four-head grouped mainline but switched the eval prompt marker from raw to normalized (`text_source_markers=true`, `eval_text_source_variant=normalized`; training still prepends learned normalized/raw marker tokens to the duplicated text_source=both data). This was worse than the raw-marker run: asr_wer_prefix fell cleanly to 1.0 with CER 0.818, despite EOS on both prompts and perfect gt-ASR sanity. So explicit normalized/raw variant markers now look stale for the aligned mixed-text grouped branch in both obvious eval-marker choices; they do not stabilize the fragile 0.3333 win and should be pruned unless a substantially richer use of text variants appears.

Added a new mixed-text dataset mode `text_source=both_balanced` that keeps only one transcript variant per audio example (alternating normalized/raw by utterance index) so the branch still mixes both text styles without doubling the dataset size, then rebuilt the caches and tested it on the aligned four-head grouped mainline with future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This did buy many more optimizer steps (3812 / 30.5k samples versus the doubled mixed-text branch’s lower-step regime), but the primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 and gt-ASR sanity weakened, even though EOS fired on both prompts and the OOD utterance shortened to 27 frames. So the mixed-text gain does not come simply from preserving both text styles while keeping the original dataset size; this first balanced one-variant-per-audio formulation now looks stale.

Retested the new `text_source=both_balanced` dataset mode under the only live stabilizer on the mixed-text aligned grouped branch by adding fuller LoRA targets (`lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`) on top of the aligned four-head grouped recipe. Even with the stronger LoRA fallback and the higher-step balanced mixed-text dataset (3650 steps / 29.2k samples), the primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, though CER stayed moderate at 0.727 and EOS fired on both prompts. So the balanced one-variant-per-audio mixed-text formulation is now stale even when paired with the reproducible fuller-LoRA fallback; it should be reverted and pruned.

Added a new cheap grouped->exact transition flag (`semantic_block_decode_overlap_last_boundary_token=true`) that makes the final token of the last grouped step provisional: it is written into the sequence but not committed to the cache, so the first exact fallback step overwrites that same boundary token. This was meant to realize the long-lived ‘corrective overlap without a full second pass’ idea on the aligned mixed-text four-head grouped mainline (`text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix). The run stayed fast enough, preserved EOS on both prompts, and kept short OOD length (~34 frames), but the primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with weak gt-ASR sanity. So this first one-token boundary-overlap implementation looks stale and should be reverted unless a richer corrective-overlap design is tried later.

Tested whether broader LoRA adaptation can rescue the tokenizer-aligned mixed-text grouped branch after shrinking Stage-1 future depth back down to the old two-head grouped setup: `text_source=both`, `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This recovered some life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with perfect gt-ASR sanity and short OOD generations, but it stayed well below both the fragile 0.3333 winner and the reproducible 0.6667 fuller-LoRA four-head fallback, while in-domain EOS disappeared and the in-domain utterance stretched back to the 53-frame cap. So fuller LoRA does not revive the older two-head grouped family on the aligned mixed-text branch; the stronger live fallback still wants four future heads.

Tested whether the fragile mixed-text aligned four-head grouped winner is update-limited by lowering `batch_size` from the default to 4 while keeping `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This did raise the step count to 3293 in 10 minutes, but because throughput dropped it only processed 13.2k samples and the primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with worse CER 0.818. So simply buying more optimizer updates does not stabilize the mixed-text aligned grouped branch; this path is another stale local retune rather than a hidden step-limited win.

Added back a stricter train/infer-match future-head supervision flag (`semantic_future_prediction_match_block_decode=true`) and tested it for the first time on the aligned mixed-text four-head grouped mainline, where inference only consumes the first two future heads on every third early-Q0 position. The run kept `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, but masked future-head training down to the grouped-step source positions and head offsets actually used by that plain grouped decode policy. This preserved EOS on both prompts and perfect gt-ASR sanity, but the primary metric still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727. So the stricter grouped-step-matched future-head mask is now stale on the aligned four-head grouped backbone too; keeping all four future heads active as a broader auxiliary still looks better than literally matching the plain grouped inference uses.

Retried the new grouped-step-matched future-head supervision mask on the aligned mixed-text four-head grouped mainline (`semantic_future_prediction_match_block_decode=true` with `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix). The rerun confirmed the first result was not a fluke in the good direction: it again landed at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, this time with worse CER 0.909, OOD EOS disappearing, and the OOD utterance stretching back to the 53-frame cap despite very strong in-domain WER 0.111. So the stricter grouped-step-matched future-head mask is now clearly stale/noisy on the aligned four-head grouped backbone and should be reverted rather than treated as a live stabilizer.

Retuned the fragile mixed-text aligned four-head grouped mainline to a shorter plain grouped prefix by reducing `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens` from 12 down to 9 while keeping `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and `semantic_block_decode_size=3`. This was a clean check of whether the live branch prefers exactly three grouped steps before exact fallback rather than four, but it still regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite preserving EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, and short generations. So the aligned mixed-text grouped branch does not simply want a shorter 9-token grouped window; the good behavior remains tightly localized elsewhere.

Tried a fresh future-head architecture change on the aligned four-head grouped backbone: `semantic_future_prediction_use_shared_audio_head=true` makes each future-Q0 head predict a transformed hidden state, then reuses the shared Stage-1 audio LM head to map that state to Q0 logits instead of having each future head own a separate direct CODEBOOK-size output matrix. On the tokenizer-aligned grouped mainline (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix), this ran cleanly and kept EOS on both prompts, but the primary metric regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with worse CER 0.864. So this first shared-audio-head future-prediction design looks stale on the reproducible aligned grouped backbone rather than like a cheaper or better speculative-token head.

Tested whether a text-prefix LM preservation loss can stabilize the fragile mixed-text aligned four-head grouped winner by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This ran cleanly but throughput dropped heavily to only 1725 steps / 13.8k samples in 10 minutes, and the primary metric regressed all the way to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with worse CER 0.909. So the simple text-prefix LM anchor does not rescue the aligned mixed-text grouped branch either; it should now be treated as stale on this newer branch too, not just on the older baselines.

Tested whether protecting the fragile mixed-text aligned grouped semantic branch from Stage-2 feedback helps by setting `detach_semantic_hidden_for_stage2=true` on top of `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, OOD EOS disappeared, the OOD utterance stretched back to the 53-frame cap, and CER worsened to 0.955. So simple Stage-2 gradient detachment is now stale on the aligned mixed-text grouped branch too; it does not stabilize the current live win.

Tested whether emphasizing Stage 1 can improve the most reproducible mixed-text aligned grouped fallback by combining the fuller LoRA target set (`lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`) with `semantic_loss_weight=2.0` and `acoustic_loss_weight=0.5` on top of `text_source=both`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, tokenizer/model-vocab alignment fix, and the 12-token 3-way grouped prefix. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0: OOD EOS disappeared, the OOD utterance hit the 53-frame cap again, gt-ASR sanity weakened, and in-domain WER worsened badly. So simple Stage-1 upweighting / Stage-2 downweighting is now stale on the aligned mixed-text fuller-LoRA fallback too; it is not a good stabilizer for the current live branch.

Tried a new future-head architecture on the reproducible tokenizer-aligned four-head grouped backbone: `semantic_future_prediction_residual_audio_head=true` makes each future-Q0 head predict a residual hidden-state delta, then reuses the shared Stage-1 audio LM head on hidden+delta instead of giving each future head an independent direct CODEBOOK-size logit matrix. On the aligned 12-token 3-way grouped mainline (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap), this ran cleanly and stayed reasonably fast at 2246 steps / 18.0k samples, but the primary metric still regressed to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727. So this first residual-audio-head future prediction design looks stale on the aligned grouped backbone too; tying future heads more tightly to the exact audio head did not rescue the grouped-prefix branch.

Added a new mixed-text ratio experiment path `text_source=both_plus_normalized`, which keeps both transcript variants but oversamples normalized text 2:1 over raw on the aligned four-head grouped mainline. The first benchmark attempt crashed before training because this new `text_source` invalidated the cached `train` / `unpadded` datasets and `./autoresearch.sh` requires them to be pre-generated with `--launcher dataset` first. Need to build the new dataset caches, then rerun the actual benchmark before judging whether this weighted mixed-text ratio is live or stale.

Added a new weighted mixed-text dataset mode `text_source=both_plus_normalized`, which keeps both transcript variants but oversamples normalized text 2:1 over raw instead of the old 1:1 doubled mixture. On the tokenizer-aligned four-head grouped-prefix branch (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, p95-style cap), this materially improved over the strongest reproducible 0.6667 backbone: final asr_wer_prefix reached 0.5 with strong CER 0.409, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~30/40 frames), and much higher training throughput at 3795 steps / 30.4k samples in 10 minutes. This still does not beat the fragile 0.3333 mixed-text outlier, but it is a new live middle ground between normalized-only stability and the noisier 1:1 mixed-text branch, so it is worth keeping and rerunning.

Exact reran the new weighted mixed-text branch `text_source=both_plus_normalized` on the tokenizer-aligned four-head grouped-prefix setup (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, p95-style cap). The promising first run at asr_wer_prefix=0.5 did not hold: the rerun fell all the way back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727, even though EOS still fired on both prompts and throughput stayed very high at 3818 steps / 30.5k samples. So the 2:1 normalized/raw mix is now a live but clearly fragile clue rather than a new reproducible backbone; it needs stabilization just like the earlier 1:1 mixed-text spike.

Tested whether broader LoRA adaptation can stabilize the new weighted mixed-text branch by combining `text_source=both_plus_normalized` with the reproducible fuller target set `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, p95-style cap). This stayed fairly fast at 3642 steps / 29.1k samples and preserved EOS on both prompts, but the primary metric only reached asr_wer_prefix=0.8333. So fuller LoRA does not stabilize the new 2:1 normalized/raw mix; it remains worse than the old reproducible 0.6667 fuller-LoRA fallback on the 1:1 mixed-text branch.

Mapped the weighted mixed-text ratio in the opposite direction by adding `text_source=both_plus_raw`, which oversamples raw transcripts 2:1 over normalized on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, p95-style cap). Unlike the new 2:1 normalized-heavy mix clue, this raw-heavy variant failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with weaker gt-ASR sanity and only middling CER 0.864. So the live weighted-mix direction is specifically toward *more normalized than raw*, not more raw; the `both_plus_raw` path should be pruned.

Generalized the new weighted mixed-text clue into a reusable `text_source=both_weighted` path with explicit repeat counts, then tested a milder 3:1 normalized/raw mixture (`text_source_weighted_normalized_repeats=3`, `text_source_weighted_raw_repeats=1`) on the tokenizer-aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe (future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token 3-way grouped prefix, p95-style cap). This again improved over the reproducible 0.6667 backbone, landing at asr_wer_prefix=0.5 with EOS on both prompts, short generations (~32/37 frames), and high throughput at 3812 steps / 30.5k samples in 10 minutes. It still does not beat the fragile 0.3333 outlier, but it suggests the live weighted-mix direction may extend beyond the hardcoded 2:1 normalized/raw branch, so this generic weighted-text infrastructure is worth keeping and rerunning.

Exact reran the new generic weighted-text branch at a 3:1 normalized/raw mix (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. The promising first run at asr_wer_prefix=0.5 did not fully hold: the rerun dropped to asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.727, though it still preserved EOS on both prompts, short generations, and high throughput at 3816 steps / 30.5k samples. So the 3:1 weighted mix is still another fragile mixed-text clue rather than a reproducible new backbone, but it may be slightly less catastrophic on rerun than the earlier hardcoded 2:1 mix, which fell all the way back to 1.0.

Swept the new generic weighted-text branch further toward normalized text by testing a 4:1 normalized/raw mixture (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 4, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This landed at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.591: better than the recent 1.0 collapses, but only back at the old reproducible baseline rather than above it. So pushing the weighted mix too far toward normalized text seems to wash out the extra mixed-text gain; the more interesting live region still looks closer to the 2:1–3:1 normalized/raw range.

Tested whether broader LoRA adaptation can stabilize the new generic weighted-text branch by combining the 3:1 normalized/raw mix (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) with the fuller target set `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This stayed reasonably fast at 3657 steps / 29.3k samples and preserved EOS on both prompts, but the primary metric collapsed to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 1.0. So fuller LoRA is not the right stabilizer for the 3:1 weighted-text branch either; the generic weighted mix clue still does not combine well with the old reproducible LoRA fallback.

Probed the new generic weighted-text branch closer to the original 1:1 mixed-text ratio by testing a 3:2 normalized/raw mix (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 2) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This stayed fast at 3819 steps / 30.6k samples and preserved EOS on both prompts, but the primary metric only reached asr_wer_prefix=0.8333. So the weighted-mix sweet spot does not seem to live near this milder 3:2 ratio; the better signals still appear more concentrated in the 2:1–3:1 normalized-heavy range.

Pushed the generic weighted-text branch to an intermediate 5:2 normalized/raw mix (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 5, raw repeats 2) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. Despite preserving EOS on both prompts and even hitting perfect in-domain WER 0.0, the OOD primary metric regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.818 and slightly weaker gt-ASR sanity. So the weighted-mix sweet spot is not simply any ratio between 2:1 and 3:1; this 5:2 point already looks like another in-domain-only side effect rather than a robust improvement.

Added a new `text_source=both_weighted_balanced` path to test whether the live normalized-heavy weighted mix stabilizes better when each utterance keeps only one transcript variant instead of literal dataset duplication. The first probe used a 3:1 normalized/raw assignment by utterance index on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe, preserving the same weighted ratio as the earlier duplicated 3:1 branch while restoring the dataset to one text variant per audio. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.955, so the weighted-mix clue does not seem to come from duplication-free one-variant balancing; the first balanced weighted formulation should be reverted and pruned.

Ran a third independent benchmark of the live 3:1 normalized/raw weighted-text branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe to measure whether its earlier 0.5->0.8333 behavior was just pure collapse or a broader distribution. This third run landed in the middle at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, and the usual high throughput (~30.4k samples / 10 min). So the 3:1 weighted mix is still clearly fragile, but it now looks less catastrophically unstable than the first 2:1 weighted branch: across three runs it has produced 0.5, 0.8333, and 0.6667 rather than collapsing twice to 1.0.

Exact reran the 4:1 normalized/raw weighted-text branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 4, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. Its first run had landed at the old reproducible 0.6667 level, but the rerun collapsed all the way back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.864. So 4:1 normalized-heavy weighting is not a reproducible stable alternative; it now looks like another fragile weighted side branch rather than a reliable fallback.

Tested the only still-somewhat-live late-boundary schedule clue on top of the weighted 3:1 normalized/raw branch by replacing the plain 12-token grouped prefix with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2` while keeping the aligned four-head future-Q0 recipe and `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1). This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite decent CER 0.636 and healthy throughput, so the late-boundary schedule clue does not rescue or stabilize the weighted-text branch either.

Tested the generic weighted-text infrastructure at the old 2:1 normalized/raw ratio (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 2, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This first reusable 2:1 run landed at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.773, EOS on both prompts, and the usual high throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min). So the old 2:1 clue was not unique to the hardcoded path, but it still looks weaker and more collapse-prone than the newer 3:1 branch: across the 2:1 family we now have 0.5, 1.0, and 0.8333 rather than a clear reproducible improvement.

Tested whether the live 3:1 normalized/raw weighted-text clue is actually specific to grouped-prefix decoding by ablating the grouped prefix entirely: kept the aligned four-head future-Q0 recipe with exact token-by-token semantic decoding, but switched text conditioning to `text_source=both_weighted` with normalized repeats 3 and raw repeats 1. Surprisingly this tied the old robust best at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, and even slightly higher throughput (~30.6k samples / 10 min). So normalized-heavy weighted text is not purely a grouped-prefix-only effect; it can also materially rescue the aligned exact-decoding backbone.

Exact reran the new weighted-text transfer onto the aligned four-head exact-decoding backbone (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, no grouped prefix). The first run had surprisingly tied the robust 0.6667 baseline, but the rerun collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with worse CER 0.818 despite very strong in-domain WER 0.222 and EOS on both prompts. So normalized-heavy weighted text is not purely grouped-specific, but its transfer to the exact-decoding backbone is also fragile rather than a new reproducible mainline.

Mapped the exact-decoding transfer further by testing the generic 2:1 normalized/raw weighted mix (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 2, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head future-Q0 backbone with no grouped prefix. Unlike the one noisy 3:1 exact-decoding success, this 2:1 transfer failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.773, despite very strong in-domain WER 0.222 and EOS on both prompts. So the exact-decoding weighted-text clue, if real at all, is at least narrower than just 'any normalized-heavy mix' and currently looks centered closer to 3:1 than 2:1.

Extended the exact-decoding weighted-text map farther toward normalized text by testing `text_source=both_weighted` with repeats 4/1 on the aligned four-head future-Q0 backbone and no grouped prefix. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727, so the one 3:1 exact-decoding hit does not extend monotonically toward more normalized-heavy weighting. The exact-decoding weighted clue now looks very narrow and noisy rather than like a general benefit from simply increasing normalized-text mass.

Ran a fourth independent benchmark of the live grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted-text branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This time it landed at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.909, EOS on both prompts, and very strong in-domain WER 0.222. That is not a new primary win, but it matters for the stability map: across four grouped 3:1 runs this branch has now produced 0.5, 0.8333, 0.6667, and 0.8333 — still fragile, but notably it has stayed sub-1.0 on every independent run so far, which makes it look more reproducible than the neighboring 2:1 family.

Ran a third independent benchmark of the weighted 3:1 normalized/raw transfer onto the aligned four-head exact-decoding backbone (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, no grouped prefix). After one initial 0.6667 hit and one collapse to 1.0, this third run also fell back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.909 and longer OOD output (45 frames). So the exact-decoding weighted-text clue now looks mostly stale/noisy: it has produced one tie-level hit followed by two clean collapses, unlike the grouped 3:1 branch which has stayed sub-1.0 on every rerun so far.

Tested whether the live grouped 3:1 weighted branch prefers a much shorter speculative window by reducing `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens` from 12 to 6 while keeping `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This stayed sub-1.0 at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with EOS on both prompts, but it did not improve on the 12-token branch and it cut 10-minute throughput noticeably to 2915 steps / 23.3k samples. So the weighted 3:1 clue does not obviously want a much shorter grouped prefix; the original 12-token window still looks at least as good and more sample-efficient.

Tested a short late-bridge schedule on the live weighted 3:1 grouped branch by replacing the plain grouped prefix with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,1,2` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) and the aligned four-head future-Q0 recipe. This regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.864 and low throughput (~23.3k samples / 10 min). So the late-boundary bridge+taper family still does not rescue the weighted 3:1 branch even when shortened to a 6-token schedule.

Mapped the minimum useful grouped-prefix length on the live weighted 3:1 branch by shrinking `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens` all the way to 3, i.e. only one grouped 3-token speculative step before exact decoding. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.818 despite very strong in-domain WER 0.111 and EOS on both prompts. So the weighted 3:1 clue does not survive with only a single grouped step; it appears to need a longer grouped prefix than 3 tokens, and probably longer than the slower 6-token retune as well.

Ran a fifth independent benchmark of the live grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted-text branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This again stayed sub-1.0 at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min). That matters more than the raw score: across five independent grouped runs, the 3:1 weighted branch has now produced 0.5, 0.8333, 0.6667, 0.8333, and 0.8333 — still not a new best, but much more reproducible than the neighboring weighted variants and much less collapse-prone than the exact-decoding transfer.

Tested whether the historical exact-last grouped transition can stabilize the live weighted 3:1 grouped branch by setting `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true` on top of `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe with a 12-token grouped window. This regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.864, slower throughput (~30.0k vs ~30.5k samples / 10 min), and no secondary upside. So exact-last does not rescue the weighted 3:1 branch either; this transition family should now be treated as stale for the weighted path too.

Mapped the grouped-prefix length on the live weighted 3:1 branch at an intermediate cutoff by testing `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=9` with `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.818 despite healthy throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min) and EOS on both prompts. So the weighted 3:1 branch now looks sharply localized around the original 12-token grouped prefix: 3 and 9 both collapse to 1.0, while 6 only reaches 0.8333 more slowly.

Extended the grouped-prefix length map on the live weighted 3:1 branch by testing a longer `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=15` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This also regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.864, so the weighted 3:1 branch now looks sharply localized around the original 12-token grouped window: 3, 9, and 15 all collapse to 1.0, while 6 only reaches 0.8333 more slowly.

Tested whether emphasizing Stage 1 can stabilize the live grouped 3:1 weighted branch by setting `semantic_loss_weight=2.0` and `acoustic_loss_weight=0.5` on top of `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while cutting 10-minute sample coverage sharply to 18.1k examples, so simple Stage-1 upweighting / Stage-2 downweighting is stale for the weighted branch too.

Tested whether a text-prefix LM anchor can stabilize the live grouped 3:1 weighted branch by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of `text_source=both_weighted` (normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This stayed sub-1.0 at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with improved CER 0.636, but it cut 10-minute sample coverage to only 23.0k examples and in-domain WER regressed to 1.0. So a simple text-loss anchor is at best a weak weighted-branch side clue, not a clear stabilizer or new mainline.

Ran a sixth independent benchmark of the live grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted-text branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after mapping several failed nearby retunes. This again stayed sub-1.0 at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333, and this time CER improved sharply to 0.545 with in-domain WER 0.333 and full throughput (~30.3k samples / 10 min). Across six independent grouped runs, the 3:1 weighted branch has now produced 0.5, 0.8333, 0.6667, 0.8333, 0.8333, and 0.8333 — still not a new best, but clearly the most reproducible weighted branch we have.

Exact reran the weighted-branch text-anchor side clue by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of the live grouped 3:1 normalized/raw branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe). The first run had stayed at 0.8333 with better CER, but this rerun collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite the same large throughput penalty (~22.9k samples / 10 min). So the weighted-branch text-loss anchor now looks noisy rather than stabilizing; it is not a reproducible improvement path in its current form.

Tested whether a milder text-prefix LM anchor could preserve the one-off CER gain on the live grouped 3:1 weighted branch by lowering the anchor to `text_loss_weight=0.05`. This failed badly: asr_wer_prefix regressed to 1.0, CER worsened to 0.955, and throughput collapsed further to only 13.7k samples / 10 min. So the weighted-branch text-loss anchor looks stale/noisy in this neighborhood rather than like a usable stabilizer.

Ran a seventh independent benchmark of the plain live grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe, 12-token grouped window) after the latest side-branch failures. This time it finally collapsed to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.682, even though EOS still fired on both prompts and throughput stayed normal (~30.3k samples / 10 min). So the branch is still the strongest weighted family overall, but it is no longer true that it has stayed sub-1.0 on every grouped rerun; the stability story needs to be downgraded from 'never collapsed' to 'more reproducible than neighbors, but still capable of full collapse.'

Ran an eighth independent benchmark of the plain live grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe, 12-token grouped window) immediately after its first full collapse. The branch bounced back strongly: asr_wer_prefix returned to 0.5 with excellent CER 0.455, EOS on both prompts, and normal throughput (~30.3k samples / 10 min). So the stability picture is now clearer: the grouped 3:1 weighted branch is definitely still noisy, but it remains the strongest weighted family overall, with a distribution that now includes two 0.5 hits, one 0.6667, four 0.8333s, and one 1.0 collapse rather than a simple monotonic decay.

Exact reran the revived grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted branch immediately after its new 0.5 rebound (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe, 12-token grouped window). The branch collapsed again to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, OOD EOS disappeared, and the OOD utterance stretched back to the 53-frame cap. So the weighted 3:1 branch remains the strongest weighted family overall, but its distribution is now clearly bimodal/noisy rather than merely softening around 0.8333.

Ran a tenth independent benchmark of the plain grouped 3:1 normalized/raw weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized repeats 3, raw repeats 1, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe, 12-token grouped window) after discarding the new sampled-pass reformulation. The plain branch recovered again to asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.636, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~32/37 frames), and full throughput (~30.6k samples / 10 min). This matters more than the raw score: after the fresh 1.0 failure and the sampled-pass dead end, the original fixed-duplicate 3:1 weighted recipe is still clearly the strongest weighted family overall. Its grouped-run distribution is now two 0.5 hits, one 0.6667, five 0.8333s, and three 1.0 collapses, and the newest failures show at least two distinct failure modes rather than a single EOS-length problem.

Tested a new weighted-text stochastic-mass variant on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe: added `text_source=both_weighted_sampled` with a new `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, so the model keeps the same 3:1 normalized/raw weighting but only builds two deterministic sampled passes instead of the full four-pass duplicate mass. This is the first version that truly decouples weighted sampling probability from total dataset mass within the 10-minute budget. It paid off immediately: asr_wer_prefix improved to 0.5 with excellent CER 0.455, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~30/40 frames), and full throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min). So the weighted clue is not limited to the original fixed-duplicate formulation after all; a lower-mass two-pass sampled 3:1 variant is now a live structural branch worth rerunning.

Ran a third benchmark of the new lower-mass sampled 3:1 weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`) after mapping its immediate neighbors. The branch bounced back to `asr_wer_prefix=0.5` again with CER 0.500, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~33/39 frames), and full throughput (~30.3k samples / 10 min). This matters because the surrounding sampled-mass neighborhood is now sharply pruned: 1 pass failed at 1.0, 3 passes failed at 1.0, and the older full 4-pass sampled formulation had already failed at 1.0. So the live sampled weighted branch now looks narrowly centered on the 2-pass setting, with an observed distribution of 0.5, 0.6667, and 0.5 rather than a one-off spike.

Mapped the weighted ratio inside the live sampled 2-pass family by lowering `text_source=both_weighted_sampled` from 3:1 to 2:1 normalized/raw while keeping `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2` and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, so the sampled branch does not broaden toward the milder 2:1 ratio.

Mapped the other side of the live sampled 2-pass family by pushing `text_source=both_weighted_sampled` from 3:1 to 4:1 normalized/raw at `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This also failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, so the sampled branch is not simply improved by pushing farther toward normalized text either.

Reran the plain live sampled branch (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after mapping nearby ratios. This time it collapsed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, showing the new sampled branch is real but not yet a reliably sub-1.0 backbone.

Tested whether the live sampled 2-pass 3:1 weighted clue transfers off the grouped-prefix decoder by ablating grouped decoding and running the same `text_source=both_weighted_sampled` branch on the aligned four-head exact-decoding backbone. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, so the sampled stochastic-mass clue currently looks grouped-prefix-specific.

Ran another grouped-prefix benchmark of the plain sampled 2-pass 3:1 branch (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`). It recovered only to asr_wer_prefix=0.8333, which was better than a full collapse but still worse than the earlier 0.5/0.6667 hits, so the branch remains noisy rather than stable.

Probed assignment sensitivity inside the live sampled branch by reintroducing a deterministic sampled-assignment salt and rerunning the same grouped-prefix `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, 3:1, 2-pass recipe at an alternate seed. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, so the branch is not just pass-count- and ratio-sensitive; the specific utterance/pass assignment also matters.

Ran yet another plain grouped-prefix rerun of the live sampled 2-pass 3:1 branch after the first alternate-seed failure. This also fell back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, further confirming that the sampled branch is bimodal/noisy rather than a settled new baseline.

Retried the assignment-sensitivity probe on the live sampled grouped-prefix branch with a second alternate deterministic sampled-assignment seed. This also failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite good in-domain behavior, sharpening the conclusion that the current sampled clue is tightly localized around the original seed-0 assignment.

Tested a new coverage-aware sampled weighted-text variant on the live sampled grouped-prefix branch: added `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_noreplace`, which keeps the same 3:1 normalized/raw weighting and 2-pass total mass as the live sampled recipe but samples without replacement inside each deterministic weighted cycle so 3:1 no longer produces double-raw example assignments. This did not help. The grouped-prefix run landed at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with EOS on both prompts, short generations (~33/38 frames), full throughput (~30.4k samples / 10 min), and decent in-domain WER 0.444, but it was clearly worse than the seed-0 sampled-with-replacement branch. So the live sampled clue does not seem to come from simply removing double-raw assignments or enforcing per-cycle weighted coverage.

Tested whether the reproducible fuller-LoRA fallback can stabilize the live 2-pass sampled 3:1 grouped-prefix branch by keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, and the aligned four-head grouped recipe but expanding LoRA targets to `[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with EOS on both prompts, short generations (~32/37 frames), slightly lower throughput (~29.1k samples / 10 min), and degraded gt-ASR sanity 0.118. So fuller LoRA does not rescue the narrow sampled stochastic-mass branch either; like the older fixed-duplicate weighted family, this sampled path also seems to prefer the plain default LoRA target set.

Tested a new low-mass anchored weighted-text variant on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe: added `text_source=both_plus_normalized_balanced`, which keeps only two total passes by combining one full normalized pass with one balanced normalized/raw pass for an overall 3:1 normalized/raw mass. This was meant to keep the sampled branch's low total mass while forcing every utterance to retain at least one normalized anchor and removing raw-only assignments. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, ~42/38 frames, and full throughput (~30.2k samples / 10 min). So the sampled 2-pass clue is not explained by a simple normalized-anchor-plus-balanced-raw formulation either.

Found a new live stabilizer inside the narrow sampled stochastic-mass branch by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This tied the best recorded overall result at asr_wer_prefix=0.3333 with excellent CER 0.318, EOS on both prompts, and short generations (~34/41 frames). The tradeoff is the same one seen on older text-loss branches: throughput dropped sharply to ~23.0k samples / 10 min and in-domain WER stayed mediocre at 0.667. So `text_loss_weight=0.1` is still not a generic upgrade, but it is now a genuinely live sampled-branch-specific stabilizer worth exact rerun.

Exact reran the new sampled-branch text-anchor keep (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `text_loss_weight=0.1`, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe). The first run had tied the overall best at 0.3333, but this rerun collapsed hard to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with catastrophic full-WER/CER while throughput stayed similarly low at ~22.9k samples / 10 min. So the `text_loss_weight=0.1` rescue is real but highly fragile: it currently looks like another sharp sampled-branch spike rather than a reproducible new mainline.

Tested whether the sampled-branch text-anchor spike prefers a milder setting by lowering the new sampled recipe to `text_loss_weight=0.05` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with OOD EOS disappearing again and the utterance stretching back to the 53-frame cap, while throughput stayed in the same slowed regime at ~22.9k samples / 10 min. So the sampled text-anchor clue is not a broad low-weight family; if there is any real gain here, it currently looks specific to the fragile `text_loss_weight=0.1` spike.

Tested whether the sampled-branch text-anchor spike extends to a slightly milder nearby setting by using `text_loss_weight=0.08` on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This also failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, with throughput collapsing even harder to only ~13.4k samples / 10 min despite EOS on both prompts. So the sampled text-anchor clue is not broad even in the immediate neighborhood below 0.1; the gain still looks like a very sharp `text_loss_weight=0.1` spike.

Tested the upper side of the sampled text-anchor spike by raising the same sampled recipe to `text_loss_weight=0.12` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This also failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, with throughput collapsing to only ~13.3k samples / 10 min. So the sampled text-anchor clue is not broad above 0.1 either; the current picture is now a very sharp isolated `text_loss_weight=0.1` spike rather than a stable local family.

Tested whether the sampled text-anchor spike can be stabilized by recovering sample coverage with a larger batch: reran the sampled `text_loss_weight=0.1` recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe) at `batch_size=12`. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite higher instantaneous samples/sec, and total 10-minute coverage still fell to only ~18.6k samples because step count collapsed to 1548. So the sampled text-anchor spike does not look like a simple small-batch or step-overhead artifact; larger batch does not rescue it.

Tested a new low-mass anchored sampled-text formulation on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe: added `text_source=both_plus_normalized_sampled`, which keeps one full normalized pass plus one sampled 1:1 normalized/raw pass for a lower-mass 3:1 overall normalized/raw mixture. This was meant to preserve the sampled branch's per-example randomness while forcing every utterance to keep a normalized anchor. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.636, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~30.0k samples / 10 min), so it is clearly worse than the live 2-pass sampled 3:1 branch and far below the old 0.3333 spike. The sampled clue therefore does not seem to come from simple 'normalized anchor + random second pass' structure either.

Tested whether the live sampled 2-pass 3:1 branch depends on the specific modulo-4 assignment geometry rather than just the normalized/raw ratio by keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, and the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe but scaling the weights from `3/1` to `6/2`. This preserves the same 3:1 marginal ratio while changing the deterministic bucket structure from 4 slots to 8 slots, so example/pass assignments and cross-pass collisions change without changing total dataset mass. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite full throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, short generations (~38/38 frames), and very strong in-domain WER 0.111. So the sampled branch is not just about the coarse 3:1 ratio and 2-pass mass; it is also sensitive to the exact assignment geometry, and the original `3/1` modulo-4 formulation still looks like another narrow local spike.

Tested a new content-aware low-mass mixed-text branch on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe by adding `text_source=normalized_plus_raw_diff`, which keeps one normalized pass for every utterance and adds a raw-text copy only for the ~11.5% of LJSpeech samples where `text_raw` actually differs from `text_norm`. This was meant to stop wasting mixed-text mass on utterances where raw and normalized text are identical while still concentrating raw exposure on the informative numeric/punctuation subset. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with OOD EOS disappearing again, the utterance stretching back to the 53-frame cap, CER 0.909, and no throughput gain beyond the usual ~30.0k samples / 10 min. So the mixed-text clue is not explained by simply targeting raw text only to the subset where normalization changes the transcript; low-mass content-aware raw exposure alone is not enough.

Retuned the new content-aware mixed-text branch to restore the raw mass of the live 3:1 family by keeping `text_source=normalized_plus_raw_diff` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe but raising `text_source_weighted_raw_repeats=3`. This keeps one normalized pass for every utterance, then adds three extra raw copies only for the ~11.5% of utterances where raw and normalized text actually differ, giving a low-mass but much more concentrated analogue of the old 3:1 normalized/raw branch. It still failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with OOD EOS disappearing again, the utterance hitting the 53-frame cap, CER 0.955, and only middling in-domain WER 0.222. So even when the informative raw subset is upweighted back toward the old 3:1 raw mass, content-aware 'raw only where text changes' targeting does not recover the mixed-text gain; the live clue is not just about concentrating raw exposure on normalization-different utterances.

Ran another plain reproducibility check of the live sampled branch on the original `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw `3/1`, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after pruning more local neighbors. It collapsed again to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, but this time it still emitted EOS on both prompts, stayed short (~40/44 frames), and kept decent CER 0.545 rather than failing via a long no-EOS cap hit. So the plain sampled branch remains real but increasingly looks bimodal with multiple failure modes; another fresh seed-0 rerun did not restore the old 0.5/0.6667 life.

Tested whether more aggressive short-example focusing helps fast convergence by lowering `length_percentile` from `0.95` to `0.9` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix backbone (`text_source=normalized`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix). This rebuilt the train set with shorter padding limits (`max_seq_len=150`, `max_audio_frames=117`) and filtered the corpus from 13.1k to 11.7k examples, but it backfired badly: throughput collapsed to only ~17.8k samples / 10 min, the run regressed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, and both prompts became under-generated despite EOS still firing (only ~28/38 frames). So simply truncating training to the 90th-length-percentile regime is stale; the benchmark seems to prefer the broader 95th-percentile coverage rather than a smaller faster-focus subset.

Tried probing an untested eval-only acoustic knob on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix backbone by setting `residual_gen_temperature=0.2` and `residual_gen_top_p=0.9` to sample Mimi residual codebooks during heavy eval instead of greedy argmax. The run crashed before training because this config invalidated the dataset hash and `./autoresearch.sh` was launched without pre-generated caches for the new config. This suggests the current dataset cache key is broader than just true dataset-building fields, so even eval-only generation changes may require a `--launcher dataset` prebuild before benchmarking.

After pre-generating caches for the broader config hash, tested an untried eval-only acoustic knob on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix backbone by setting `residual_gen_temperature=0.2` and `residual_gen_top_p=0.9` so Mimi residual codebooks are sampled instead of generated by greedy argmax. This was clearly harmful: asr_wer_prefix stayed at 1.0, CER exploded to 11.45, in-domain WER regressed to 0.889, and sample coverage dropped to only ~22.4k / 10 min even though EOS still fired on both prompts. So residual-codebook sampling during eval is a bad direction in this regime; the benchmark strongly prefers greedy residual generation.

Tested whether the aligned four-head grouped-prefix backbone is acoustically under-capacity by enlarging Stage 2 from 4 to 6 residual-transformer layers while keeping the rest of the robust recipe fixed (`text_source=normalized`, future steps 4, future weight 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_prob=0.5`, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix). This increased the model/optimizer slightly but hurt the 10-minute regime: sample coverage fell to only ~17.1k examples, OOD EOS disappeared again, the utterance hit the 53-frame cap, and asr_wer_prefix stayed at 1.0. So simply making Stage 2 deeper is not a fast-convergence win; the benchmark remains more sample-limited than acoustically under-capacity in this direction.

Tested whether the fixed-duplicate weighted branch depends on the exact total duplicate mass or mainly on the normalized/raw ratio by keeping the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe and preserving the same 3:1 ratio, but doubling the duplicate counts from `text_source=both_weighted` repeats `3/1` to `6/2`. This larger-mass same-ratio branch stayed alive at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with strong CER 0.545, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~34/45 frames), and full throughput (~30.2k samples / 10 min). So the fixed-duplicate weighted family is not pinned to exactly four total copies; higher duplicate mass can still produce sub-1.0 life, though it did not recover the older 0.5/0.6667 bests. This suggests the fixed duplicate branch is broader in total-mass space than the much narrower sampled branch, even if the original `3/1` recipe remains the stronger center.

Extended the new fixed-duplicate same-ratio mass sweep by pushing `text_source=both_weighted` from repeats `6/2` up to `9/3` while keeping the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe and the same 3:1 normalized/raw ratio. This improved again to asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~33/41 frames), and full throughput (~30.2k samples / 10 min). So the fixed-duplicate weighted branch does not just tolerate more total mass; at least in this first probe it actually recovers back to the robust 0.6667 level at higher same-ratio duplicate mass. That makes the fixed-duplicate family look materially broader in total-mass space than the sampled branch, and it raises the live question of whether the old `3/1` 0.5 hits came from a sharper duplicate-mass sweet spot somewhere above four total copies rather than exactly at it.

Exact reran the new higher-mass fixed-duplicate 3:1 weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, repeats `9/3`) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after its first 0.6667 hit. It collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with OOD EOS disappearing, the utterance stretching to 51 frames, and even one invalid Q0 token appearing in the OOD sample. So the larger-mass fixed-duplicate branch is real but still noisy; the new 9/3 mass level does not immediately become a reproducible upgrade over the older 3/1 branch.

Extended the fixed-duplicate same-ratio 3:1 mass sweep one step further by pushing `text_source=both_weighted` to repeats `12/4` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after the new `6/2` and `9/3` life. This time it failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite full throughput (~30.4k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, short generations (~31/39 frames), and very strong in-domain WER 0.333. So the duplicate-mass family is broader than the sampled branch, but it is not monotonically improving with more total 3:1 mass; by `12/4` the branch has already flipped into another short-EOS wrong-content failure mode rather than the older long/no-EOS collapse.

Exact reran the earlier fixed-duplicate same-ratio 3:1 weighted mass retune at repeats `6/2` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after the newer `9/3` and `12/4` mapping. It also collapsed to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite full throughput (~30.6k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, short generations (~32/38 frames), and decent in-domain WER 0.444. So the apparent broader duplicate-mass family is still high-variance across at least `6/2`, `9/3`, and `12/4`; even the milder 6/2 point does not yet show reproducible stability and its failures can happen in the same short-EOS wrong-content mode as 12/4 rather than only as long/no-EOS collapses.

Tested whether the old fragile 1:1 mixed-text grouped win can be recovered or stabilized by using the generic fixed-duplicate weighted path at equal normalized/raw mass but higher total duplicate count: `text_source=both_weighted` with repeats `2/2` on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite full throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, short generations (~28/38 frames), and decent CER 0.682. So the earlier `text_source=both` spike does not seem to generalize to a broader equal-ratio fixed-duplicate family; simply adding more total 1:1 duplicate mass is stale and lands in the same short-EOS wrong-content failure mode as several recent weighted misses.

Tested whether the older normalized-heavy 2:1 clue broadens in duplicate-mass space the same way the 3:1 family does by raising the generic fixed-duplicate weighted branch from repeats `2/1` to `4/2` while keeping the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This recovered sub-1.0 life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, very strong in-domain WER 0.111, and full throughput (~30.6k samples / 10 min), though gt-ASR sanity slipped to 0.333. So the 2:1 normalized/raw family is not completely dead outside the old hardcoded spike; higher total duplicate mass can keep it alive even if it still does not recover the old 0.5 result. This makes the broader picture more symmetric: both the 2:1 and 3:1 fixed-duplicate families have some real mass sensitivity, while the sampled branch remains much narrower and more geometry-fragile.

Exact reran the new higher-mass 2:1 fixed-duplicate weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, repeats `4/2`) on the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe after its first 0.8333 hit. It collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with EOS still firing on both prompts, short generations (~32/39 frames), and middling in-domain WER 0.556. So the 2:1 family now mirrors the 3:1 family's recent behavior: duplicate-mass retunes can produce real first-run life, but they are still noisy and can fail in the same short-EOS wrong-content mode rather than through the older long/no-EOS collapse.

Tested a new optimizer-side stabilizer on the most promising higher-mass fixed-duplicate weighted branch by lowering the global learning rate from `3e-4` to `2e-4` on `text_source=both_weighted` repeats `9/3` with the aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe. This recovered asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with EOS on the OOD prompt and 39 OOD frames, so the higher-mass 3:1 branch is not tied to the default LR. But the tradeoff was ugly: throughput collapsed to only ~18.1k samples / 10 min, the in-domain utterance hit the 53-frame cap with missing EOS, and gt-ASR sanity slipped to 0.125. So a lower global LR is a real live knob on this branch, but right now it looks more like a noisy throughput-costly side path than a clean stabilizer.

Exact reran the lower-learning-rate high-mass 3:1 weighted branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, repeats `9/3`, `learning_rate=2e-4`) after its first 0.6667 hit. It collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with EOS on both prompts, short generations (~35/38 frames), and only middling in-domain WER 0.667. So lowering the global LR does not stabilize the higher-mass 3:1 branch; like the recent mass sweeps it is another noisy first-run side path rather than a reproducible upgrade.

Tested a post-alignment transfer of the older broader exact-last clue onto the tokenizer-aligned grouped backbone by combining `text_source=normalized`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, `semantic_block_decode_size=4`, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`, and the future-slots-only cheap early-prefix match (`semantic_self_condition_use_inference_policy=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_early_grouped_prefix=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_grouped_future_slots_only=true`, `semantic_self_condition_inference_policy_prob=0.0`) with the 12-token grouped window and EOS weight 2.0. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with only 16.8k samples / 10 min and no primary upside despite decent CER 0.727. So the older block_size=4 exact/future/future/exact matched-training clue does not transfer cleanly onto the aligned normalized grouped backbone; post-alignment it mostly looks like another slow stale side path rather than a successor to the plain grouped-prefix mainline.

Tested a simple global optimizer retune on the tokenizer-aligned reproducible grouped-prefix backbone by lowering `learning_rate` from `3e-4` to `2e-4` while keeping `text_source=normalized`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the 12-token grouped prefix. It tied the robust 0.6667 level again with slightly better CER 0.636, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~31/35 frames), and full throughput (~30.4k samples / 10 min). So unlike the noisy weighted-branch transfer, lower global LR may actually be a viable stabilizer on the plain aligned grouped backbone and is worth exact rerun before treating it as stale.

Exact reran the lower-learning-rate aligned grouped backbone (`text_source=normalized`, `learning_rate=2e-4`, four future-Q0 heads, 12-token grouped prefix) after its first tie-level 0.6667 result. It collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with EOS on both prompts, normal throughput (~30.4k samples / 10 min), and no compensating secondary upside. So lowering the global LR on the plain aligned grouped backbone now looks just like the weighted-branch transfer: another noisy first-run side path rather than a reproducible stabilizer.

Tested a softer Stage-2-preserving optimizer split on the aligned grouped backbone by lowering only `residual_learning_rate` to `2e-4` while keeping the global / semantic rate at the default `3e-4` on `text_source=normalized`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the 12-token grouped prefix. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while throughput collapsed to only ~16.0k samples / 10 min, despite EOS on both prompts and short generations. So simply slowing Stage 2 through a lower residual LR is stale in this regime; it behaves more like the bad low-LR side paths than like a stabilizer.

Tested a post-alignment Stage-1 denoising retune on the aligned grouped backbone by adding `semantic_q0_corruption_prob=0.05` to `text_source=normalized`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the 12-token grouped prefix. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while total coverage dropped to only ~17.8k samples / 10 min, despite EOS on both prompts and decent in-domain WER 0.444. So mild Stage-1 token corruption now looks stale on the aligned grouped backbone too; it behaves more like another throughput-costly side path than an exposure-bias fix.

Tried a more targeted EOS-floor retune on the fragile mixed-text aligned grouped winner by setting `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=24` on `text_source=both`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the 12-token grouped prefix. The run crashed before training because this inference-only generation setting invalidated the current dataset cache key and `./autoresearch.sh` was launched without a pre-generated dataset cache for the new config. So moderate `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens` remains unjudged; like the earlier residual-generation sampling probe, it first needs a `--launcher dataset` prebuild even though it only changes eval-time generation behavior.

Retested the fragile mixed-text aligned grouped branch with a more targeted EOS-floor retune by prebuilding caches and then setting `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=24` on `text_source=both`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the 12-token grouped prefix. This recovered asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with EOS on both prompts, stronger in-domain WER 0.333, and moderate generation lengths (~42/40 frames), though throughput dropped to only ~22.9k samples / 10 min.
