So the earlier blunt 48-token floor was too aggressive, but a moderate minimum-length floor may be a real stabilizer candidate for the mixed-text aligned branch rather than pure stale EOS forcing.

Exact reran the mixed-text aligned grouped branch with the targeted EOS floor (`text_source=both`, `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=24`) after its first 0.6667 recovery. It collapsed back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while keeping EOS on both prompts, restoring full throughput (~30.2k samples / 10 min), and even improving CER to 0.591. So a moderate minimum-length floor is not yet a reproducible stabilizer for the mixed-text branch; like several recent local retunes, it may be another noisy first-run side path rather than a robust fix for the short-EOS failure mode.

Mapped the lower side of the new mixed-text EOS-floor clue by rerunning the aligned grouped `text_source=both` branch with `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=16` instead of `24`, after reusing the prebuilt cache family for that config hash. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with EOS on both prompts, ~22.9k samples / 10 min, and no secondary upside. So the moderate minimum-length-floor idea does not obviously broaden downward from 24; at least so far it looks like another narrow local spike rather than a robust mixed-text stabilizer family.

Tested whether the new moderate EOS-floor clue broadens when stacked onto the reproducible mixed-text fuller-LoRA fallback by combining `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=24` with `text_source=both`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the 12-token grouped prefix, and `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]`. It tied the fallback at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with EOS on both prompts, CER 0.636, very strong in-domain WER 0.222, and shorter generations (~37/38 frames), but throughput again dropped to only ~22.0k samples / 10 min. So the moderate minimum-length floor can coexist with the fuller-LoRA fallback, but so far it does not improve the primary metric beyond the already-reproducible 0.6667 branch.

Tested whether the new moderate EOS-floor clue transfers to the broader fixed-duplicate weighted family by prebuilding caches and then setting `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=24` on the aligned grouped 3:1 normalized/raw branch (`text_source=both_weighted`, repeats 3/1, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix). It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while keeping EOS on both prompts, full throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min), and short generations (~30/39 frames). So the moderate minimum-length floor does not rescue the live fixed-duplicate weighted branch; this family's failures are not just early-EOS truncation either.

Tested whether the moderate EOS-floor clue transfers to the live sampled stochastic-mass branch by setting `semantic_gen_min_new_tokens=24` on the aligned grouped 2-pass sampled 3:1 recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix). It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while keeping EOS on both prompts, full throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min), and short generations (~32/40 frames), though CER held at 0.500. So the moderate minimum-length floor also does not rescue the sampled weighted branch; like the fixed-duplicate branch, its failures are not explained by simple early-EOS truncation.

Implemented and tested a richer corrective-overlap grouped decoder on the aligned normalized grouped backbone: added `semantic_block_decode_overlap_last_grouped_token=true`, which makes the final token of every grouped step provisional and leaves it out of the KV cache so the next decode step overwrites that same boundary token exactly. Tested the first plausible transfer with `semantic_block_decode_size=4` and a 12-token grouped prefix, so each overlap step still commits an effective 3-token rhythm while using one extra future head before the exact overwrite. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with EOS on both prompts, short generations (~31/39 frames), and only ~23.1k samples / 10 min. So this richer per-step overlap design does not rescue the aligned grouped backbone either; the corrective-overlap family looks even weaker than the simpler exact-last / late-bridge clues.

Transferred the new per-step corrective-overlap decoder to the fragile mixed-text aligned grouped branch by keeping `text_source=both`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the grouped-prefix recipe but switching to `semantic_block_decode_size=4` plus `semantic_block_decode_overlap_last_grouped_token=true` so each grouped step commits an effective 3-token rhythm while overwriting the last boundary token exactly on the next step. This recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with EOS on both prompts, short generations (~31/39 frames), and full throughput (~30.3k samples / 10 min), but it stayed far below the reproducible 0.6667 backbone and the earlier mixed-text spikes. So this richer overlap-every-step design is at best another weak mixed-text side clue, not a successor to the plain grouped-prefix mainline.

Tested whether the late-boundary schedule clue transfers to the live sampled stochastic-mass branch by running the aligned grouped 2-pass sampled 3:1 recipe with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2` instead of the plain 12-token grouped cutoff (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0). It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while keeping EOS on both prompts, full throughput (~30.3k samples / 10 min), and short generations (~32/37 frames). So the sampled branch does not seem to want the old late-boundary bridge+taper clue either; its remaining life still looks tied to the plain grouped-prefix transition rather than to explicit schedule smoothing.

Tested whether the old exact-last grouped transition transfers to the live sampled stochastic-mass branch by running the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 recipe with `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true` (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix). Unlike the stale late-boundary sampled schedule, this recovered real life at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with strong CER 0.545, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~30/38 frames), and full throughput (~30.0k samples / 10 min), though in-domain WER stayed weak at 0.889. So the sampled branch is not tied strictly to the plain grouped-prefix transition after all: exact-last is now a live sampled-side clue worth exact rerun.

Exact reran the new sampled exact-last transition clue on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). Instead of collapsing, the rerun jumped to a new best recorded result: asr_wer_prefix=0.1667 with excellent CER 0.091, EOS on both prompts, and short generations (~28/38 frames), though gt-ASR sanity weakened to 0.263 and in-domain WER stayed only moderate at 0.556. So the sampled branch is not just a text-assignment spike after all: the exact-last transition has now become the strongest live sampled-side clue and the new best overall recipe, despite still looking potentially noisy.

Ran a third benchmark of the new sampled exact-last branch after its surprising jump to the new best 0.1667 result (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). It collapsed straight back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while keeping EOS on both prompts, short generations (~29/37 frames), and full throughput (~30.0k samples / 10 min). So the new best sampled exact-last result is real history but still highly unstable; this branch has joined the same sharp-spike category as the earlier mixed-text and sampled text-anchor wins rather than becoming a stable new mainline.

Tested whether the old sampled text-anchor spike stacks with the newly revived sampled exact-last transition by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 while throughput collapsed to just ~16.4k samples / 10 min and the in-domain prompt remained poor. So the two sampled-side spikes do not stack cleanly; exact-last plus `text_loss_weight=0.1` is worse than the plain exact-last sampled branch and does not stabilize the new 0.1667 outlier.

Tested whether the reproducible fuller-LoRA fallback can stabilize the new sampled exact-last branch by adding `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]` on top of the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). It recovered only the old 0.6667 level with CER 0.500 while throughput fell to ~20.3k samples / 10 min. So fuller LoRA does not stabilize or preserve the new 0.1667 sampled exact-last spike; like the text anchor, it only drags the branch back toward slower tie-level behavior.

Tested whether the new sampled exact-last spike is tightly pinned to the old 12-token grouped window by shortening the grouped prefix to `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=9` on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). This stayed strongly alive at asr_wer_prefix=0.3333 with CER 0.318, EOS on both prompts, and short generations (~28/37 frames), though throughput dropped to ~21.5k samples / 10 min and in-domain WER collapsed to 1.0. So unlike the older fixed-duplicate exact-last clue, the sampled exact-last branch may have some real grouped-window breadth rather than being pinned to exactly 12 tokens, even if it remains very noisy.

Exact reran the shortened-window sampled exact-last branch after its first 0.3333 keep (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`, `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=9`). It collapsed straight back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 while keeping EOS on both prompts, near-full throughput (~29.7k samples / 10 min), and even very strong in-domain WER 0.111. So the sampled exact-last 9-token branch is another noisy side spike rather than a stable shorter-window successor to the 12-token recipe.

Tested whether the sampled exact-last spike is partly a train/infer future-width mismatch by shrinking the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe from four future-Q0 heads down to `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the 12-token grouped prefix with `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`. This recovered solid life at asr_wer_prefix=0.5 with full throughput (~31.0k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, short generations (~36/33 frames), perfect gt-ASR sanity, and much stronger in-domain WER 0.333. So the sampled exact-last branch is not obviously tied to the old four-head backbone: reducing future depth toward the actual exact-last speculative width may be a real stabilizer clue rather than just a weaker regression.

Exact reran the new 2-head sampled exact-last transfer after its first 0.5 keep (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). It collapsed straight back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0, lost OOD EOS entirely, stretched the OOD utterance back to the 53-frame cap, and degraded to CER 0.955 despite keeping near-full throughput (~30.9k samples / 10 min). So reducing future depth toward the exact-last speculative width is a real first-run clue, but it is not yet a reproducible stabilizer for the sampled exact-last branch.

Tested a more directly train/infer-matched sampled exact-last transfer by keeping the live 2-pass sampled 3:1 text recipe but using `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2` together with the broader `semantic_block_decode_size=4` exact/future/future/exact decoder (`semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`, 12-token grouped prefix, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap). This recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.636, perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and very strong in-domain WER 0.111, but throughput dropped to only ~21.7k samples / 10 min. So the sampled branch does not obviously want the broader exact/future/future/exact transition either; if reduced future depth matters here, it looks more like a narrow 3-token exact-last clue than a broad block_size=4 revival.

Tested whether the new 2-head sampled exact-last clue prefers the shorter grouped window that once reached 0.3333 on the four-head branch by combining `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2` with `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=9` on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). This landed only at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.636, EOS on both prompts, short generations (~28/37 frames), and only ~22.5k samples / 10 min. So the weaker 2-head transfer does not inherit the old 9-token sampled exact-last spike; if reduced future depth is useful at all, it currently seems more compatible with the original 12-token window than with the shorter-window branch.

Tested the maximally train/infer-matched sampled exact-last variant by shrinking the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 branch all the way to `semantic_future_prediction_steps=1` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the 12-token grouped prefix with `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts and short generations (~35/42 frames), while throughput collapsed to only ~17.7k samples / 10 min and gt-ASR sanity weakened to 0.158. So fully collapsing future depth to the single speculative exact-last slot is too aggressive; the sampled branch does not want a one-head exact-last training target.

Mapped the only remaining untested interior future-depth point on the sampled exact-last branch by setting `semantic_future_prediction_steps=3` on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, but throughput collapsed to only ~17.1k samples / 10 min and the OOD utterance lengthened back out to 49 frames. So the sampled exact-last family does not seem to hide a smoother optimum at three future heads either; after probing 1, 2, 3, and 4 heads, the future-depth axis now looks mostly like a noisy side path rather than a clean stabilizer family.

Mapped a shorter grouped-window neighbor on the main sampled exact-last branch by reducing the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe from a 12-token grouped prefix down to `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=6` while keeping four future-Q0 heads, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`. This recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.591, EOS on both prompts, and only ~21.7k samples / 10 min. So the sampled exact-last family does not obviously broaden toward a much shorter 6-token grouped prefix either; the better life still appears centered around the 9–12 token region, even though that region remains highly unstable.

Mapped a longer grouped-window neighbor on the main sampled exact-last branch by extending the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe from a 12-token grouped prefix to `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=15` while keeping four future-Q0 heads, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727 despite EOS on both prompts, near-full throughput (~21.8k samples / 10 min), and short generations (~40/37 frames). So the sampled exact-last branch does not broaden cleanly to a longer grouped prefix either; taken together with the weaker 6-token result, the remaining life really does look concentrated in the narrow 9–12 token region.

Tested whether the sampled exact-last branch wants a softer exit from grouped decoding via an exact-last partial-final-block schedule by replacing the plain 12-token cutoff with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,2` on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, short generations (~30/38 frames), and only ~21.7k samples / 10 min. So the sampled exact-last family does not seem to want the old 11-token partial-final-block smoothing clue either; its remaining life still looks tied more to the hard 9–12 token cutoffs than to a softer exact-last taper.

Tested whether the sampled exact-last branch wants the older late-boundary bridge+taper clue when combined with exact-last grouped steps by running the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 recipe with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2` plus `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true` (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap). It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, short generations (~33/40 frames), and only ~21.8k samples / 10 min. So the older late-boundary schedule clue does not rescue the sampled exact-last family either; sampled schedule smoothing now looks stale both with and without exact-last.

Tested whether a cheap train/infer match can stabilize the sampled exact-last branch by enabling the future-slots-only early-prefix inference-policy replacement path on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, `semantic_self_condition_use_inference_policy=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_early_grouped_prefix=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_grouped_future_slots_only=true`, `semantic_self_condition_inference_policy_prob=0.0`, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, 12-token grouped prefix, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`). It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, lost OOD EOS, stretched back to the 53-frame cap, and cut coverage to only ~17.6k samples / 10 min. So this cheap early-prefix train/infer-match path does not rescue the sampled exact-last family either; on this branch it behaves like another stale slowdown rather than a stabilizer.

Tested whether the only mildly promising 2-head sampled exact-last side clue becomes more stable when paired with the cheap future-slots-only train/infer-match path (`semantic_self_condition_use_inference_policy=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_early_grouped_prefix=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_grouped_future_slots_only=true`, `semantic_self_condition_inference_policy_prob=0.0`) on the aligned 2-pass sampled 3:1 exact-last recipe with `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and a 12-token grouped prefix. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with worse gt-ASR sanity 0.333, only ~18.3k samples / 10 min, and an OOD utterance stretched back out to 49 frames despite EOS. So even the more width-matched 2-head sampled exact-last branch does not benefit from this cheap early-prefix train/infer-match path; that family now looks stale on sampled exact-last regardless of future depth.

Tested whether the live 2-pass sampled 3:1 branch wants future-depth matched to the plain grouped transition even without exact-last by shrinking from four future-Q0 heads down to `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2` on the aligned sampled grouped-prefix recipe (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, `semantic_block_decode_size=3`, 12-token grouped prefix, no exact-last). This recovered solid life at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with strong CER 0.545, perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, very strong in-domain WER 0.111, and full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So the sampled branch is not tied to the old four-head grouped backbone either: a cleaner two-head grouped transfer is now a real live alternative, and it looks less pathological on secondary metrics than the noisier sampled exact-last spike.

Exact reran the new 2-head sampled grouped-transfer branch after its first 0.6667 keep (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, `semantic_block_decode_size=3`, 12-token grouped prefix, no exact-last). It held the same asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min), and even stronger in-domain WER 0.0, though CER softened to 0.727. So unlike the noisier sampled exact-last family, the plain sampled grouped branch with future depth matched to the actual 3-token grouped transition now looks like a genuinely reproducible 0.6667-side backbone rather than a one-off spike.

Mapped the grouped-window breadth of the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone by shortening its grouped prefix from 12 tokens to `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=9` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and plain `semantic_block_decode_size=3` grouped decoding. This collapsed cleanly to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with exploded WER/CER despite EOS on both prompts, so the cleaner 2-head sampled grouped branch does not inherit the old 9-token sampled exact-last spike either; like the weighted grouped family, it currently still looks tightly centered on the original 12-token grouped prefix.

Tested whether the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone broadens toward a shorter grouped prefix by reducing `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens` from 12 down to 6 while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and plain `semantic_block_decode_size=3` grouped decoding. Unlike the 9-token collapse, this stayed strongly alive at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with better CER 0.500, perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and solid throughput (~23.8k samples / 10 min), though in-domain WER softened to 0.444. So the cleaner 2-head sampled grouped branch is not pinned strictly to 12 grouped tokens after all: it now looks like a broader 6–12 token family, with 9 unexpectedly acting like a bad local geometry rather than a simple monotonic width trend.

Exact reran the new 6-token variant of the 2-head sampled grouped backbone after its first 0.6667 keep (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, `semantic_block_decode_size=3`, `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens=6`, no exact-last). It collapsed straight back to asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min), and short generations (~31/36 frames). So the apparent 6-token broadening was another first-run spike; for now the genuinely reproducible sampled grouped 2-head branch still looks centered on the original 12-token grouped prefix rather than on shorter windows.

Mapped the longer grouped-window neighbor of the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone by extending `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens` from 12 to 15 while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and plain `semantic_block_decode_size=3` grouped decoding. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.909 despite EOS on both prompts and healthy throughput (~23.8k samples / 10 min). So on the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head branch, the grouped-window family still looks asymmetric and narrow: 12 is reproducibly live, 9 and 15 both fail, and the only shorter hint at 6 already collapsed on exact rerun.

Tested whether the old late-boundary schedule clue revives on the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone by replacing the plain 12-token grouped cutoff with `semantic_block_decode_schedule=3,3,3,1,2` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the p95-style cap. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.682, strong in-domain WER 0.111, and full throughput (~31.2k samples / 10 min), but it still regressed relative to the reproducible 0.6667 plain 12-token grouped branch. So the older late-boundary schedule family remains a side clue rather than a stabilizer even on the cleaner 2-head sampled grouped backbone.

Tested whether the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone broadens across normalized/raw ratios by lowering the sampled weighting from 3:1 down to `2:1` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. This recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with exploded full-WER/CER despite perfect gt-ASR sanity, strong in-domain WER 0.111, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head branch does not obviously broaden toward the older 2:1 normalized/raw neighborhood either; the live sampled grouped family still seems centered on the original 3:1 ratio.

Tested the opposite normalized-heavier neighbor on the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone by raising the sampled weighting from 3:1 up to `4:1` while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727 despite perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~31.3k samples / 10 min). So the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head branch does not broaden toward the more normalized-heavy 4:1 neighbor either; on this branch the original sampled 3:1 ratio still looks like the real center.

Tested whether the old sampled text-anchor spike transfers to the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 while throughput collapsed to just ~14.0k samples / 10 min, OOD EOS disappeared, and in-domain WER regressed to 1.0. So the old sampled text-anchor spike does not rescue the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head backbone either; on this branch it behaves like another stale slowdown rather than a stabilizer.

Tested whether the reproducible fuller-LoRA fallback transfers to the new 2-head sampled grouped backbone by adding `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]` on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 despite perfect gt-ASR and in-domain WER, while shortening the OOD utterance further and staying below the reproducible 0.6667 default-target branch. So fuller LoRA is not the right stabilizer for the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head backbone either; like the older sampled branches, this one still seems to prefer the default LoRA target set.

Tested whether the new 2-head grouped transfer improves the older fixed-duplicate weighted family by combining the aligned 3:1 fixed-duplicate text mix (`text_source=both_weighted`, normalized/raw repeats 3/1) with `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.636, EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, and full throughput (~31.1k samples / 10 min). So the cleaner 2-head grouped transfer does not rescue the older fixed-duplicate 3:1 family the way it does the sampled branch; this still looks like evidence that the stronger live clue is specifically the sampled assignment geometry rather than future-depth matching alone.

Tested whether the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone broadens toward higher sampled mass by raising `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes` from 2 to 3 while keeping the original sampled 3:1 ratio, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.636 despite perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~31.2k samples / 10 min). So even on the cleaner 2-head sampled grouped backbone, the sampled-mass axis still does not broaden cleanly above the original 2-pass center.

Tested whether the new reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone broadens toward lower sampled mass by reducing `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes` from 2 down to 1 while keeping the original sampled 3:1 ratio, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with strong CER 0.545, EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, and full throughput (~31.3k samples / 10 min), but still regressed relative to the reproducible 2-pass center. So even on the cleaner 2-head sampled grouped backbone, the sampled-mass axis still looks centered on exactly 2 passes rather than broadening toward either 1 or 3 passes.

Tested whether the sampled branch's remaining signal can be stabilized at eval by letting heavy eval choose between the plain grouped and exact-last transitions on the reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, plain 12-token grouped prefix, plus `semantic_eval_compare_exact_last_candidate=true`). This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0, lost OOD EOS, stretched back to the 53-frame cap, and cut effective coverage to only ~20.2k samples / 10 min. So on the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head backbone, heavy-eval reranking between plain grouped and exact-last behaves like another stale slowdown rather than a usable bridge between the reproducible grouped backbone and the fragile sampled exact-last spike.

Tested whether the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone benefits from a cheap early-prefix train/infer match by enabling the future-slots-only inference-policy replacement path (`semantic_self_condition_use_inference_policy=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_early_grouped_prefix=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_grouped_future_slots_only=true`, `semantic_self_condition_inference_policy_prob=0.0`) on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.591, EOS on both prompts, and reduced effective coverage to ~20.7k samples / 10 min, while in-domain WER stayed weak and gt-ASR sanity softened. So even on the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head backbone, the cheap future-slots-only early-prefix train/infer-match path is still another stale slowdown rather than the missing stabilizer.

Tested the broader cheap early-prefix train/infer-match variant on the reproducible 2-head sampled grouped backbone by enabling early-prefix inference-policy replacement without restricting it to only speculative future slots (`semantic_self_condition_use_inference_policy=true`, `semantic_self_condition_match_early_grouped_prefix=true`, `semantic_self_condition_inference_policy_prob=0.0`) on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with only middling CER 0.682, weak in-domain WER 0.667, and coverage down to only ~18.3k samples / 10 min. So on the cleaner sampled grouped 2-head backbone, the cheap early-prefix train/infer-match family now looks stale in both forms: the whole-window version fails outright, while the future-slots-only version only recovers weak 0.8333 life.

Added a new sampled text-source variant `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` that keeps the same deterministic sampled-pass geometry as `both_weighted_sampled` but only actually uses raw text on utterances where `text_raw != text_norm`, falling back to normalized text when the two transcript forms are identical. On the reproducible sampled grouped 2-head backbone (`text_source_weighted_normalized_repeats=3`, `text_source_weighted_raw_repeats=1`, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, plain 12-token grouped prefix), this improved to asr_wer_prefix=0.5 with CER 0.500, strong in-domain WER 0.222, perfect gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So the sampled branch's gain is not coming from broad raw duplication on unchanged utterances; concentrating the raw exposure onto the true normalized-vs-raw difference subset while preserving the sampled 2-pass geometry is a real new structural clue and currently the cleanest sampled-side improvement over the reproducible 0.6667 backbone.

Exact reran the new `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` branch after its first 0.5 keep, keeping the same sampled grouped 2-head recipe: normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. The first-run gain did not hold: this rerun softened back to asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.682, weaker in-domain WER 0.444, and slightly degraded gt-ASR sanity 0.158, though EOS and full throughput (~31.3k samples / 10 min) remained intact. So concentrating raw exposure only on true normalized-vs-raw difference utterances is a real new sampled-side clue, but in its current plain 2-pass 3:1 grouped form it still looks noisy rather than yet reproducible.

Tested whether the new diff-targeted sampled text mix transfers to the fragile sampled exact-last family by swapping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` into the old best-side recipe while keeping normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, four future-Q0 heads, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, the 12-token grouped prefix, and `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, decent in-domain WER 0.333, and still-healthy throughput (~30.1k samples / 10 min). So the new raw-difference-targeted sampled geometry is not a generic upgrade across the sampled family: it helps the plain grouped 2-head backbone, but it does not rescue the fragile four-head sampled exact-last spike.

Tested whether the new diff-targeted sampled text mix helps the width-matched sampled exact-last side clue by combining `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` with normalized/raw weights 3/1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, the 12-token grouped prefix, and `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`. This also failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, strong in-domain WER 0.222, and healthy throughput (~30.9k samples / 10 min). So the new raw-difference-targeted sampled geometry appears specific to the plain grouped 2-head path; it does not transfer to either the four-head or the width-matched two-head sampled exact-last branches.

Tried to map the nominal normalized/raw ratio on the new `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` family by lowering it from 3:1 to 2:1 on the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone, but `./autoresearch.sh` crashed before training because the changed `text_source` hash / sampled-weight config required fresh `train` and `unpadded` dataset caches. Need to prebuild the 2:1 diff-targeted sampled datasets with `uv run examples/ljspeech.py --launcher dataset ...` before this ratio probe can be judged.

Retried the new diff-targeted sampled family with a nominally raw-heavier ratio by lowering `text_source_weighted_normalized_repeats:text_source_weighted_raw_repeats` from 3:1 to 2:1 on the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone, keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff`, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, this failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 despite EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min), and only middling CER 0.636. So even inside the new raw-difference-targeted sampled geometry, the nominal normalized/raw ratio still looks narrow: simply shifting it toward more raw exposure on the differing subset does not preserve the first 0.5 gain.

Tested the more normalized-heavy neighbor of the new diff-targeted sampled family by raising the nominal weighting from 3:1 up to 4:1 on `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff`, while keeping `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, it recovered only tie-level life at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.591, EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, and full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min), but it still stayed below the first-run 0.5 result of the original 3:1 diff-targeted recipe. So the new raw-difference-targeted sampled branch is real, but its nominal ratio axis already looks narrow and asymmetric: shifting raw heavier to 2:1 fails outright, while pushing farther normalized to 4:1 only softens back to the old reproducible 0.6667 level rather than improving on the 3:1 first-run gain.

Tested whether the new `both_weighted_sampled_diff` clue is mainly about concentrating raw exposure onto the true normalized-vs-raw difference subset, or whether it still depends on the sampled-pass assignment geometry, by adding a temporary fixed-duplicate `text_source=both_weighted_diff` variant and running it on the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone with nominal normalized/raw repeats 3:1, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, this recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.636, perfect in-domain WER and gt-ASR sanity, EOS on both prompts, and full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So merely restricting raw text to the differing subset is not enough by itself: the stronger 0.5 signal from `both_weighted_sampled_diff` still appears to depend on the sampled low-mass / assignment geometry rather than on a fixed-duplicate diff-only dataset alone.

Tested whether the noisy `both_weighted_sampled_diff` clue wants *more* sampled mass once raw exposure is restricted only to utterances where `text_raw != text_norm`, by raising `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes` from 2 to 4 while keeping the original 3:1 nominal weighting, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, this failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.773 despite EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, decent in-domain WER 0.222, and essentially full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So the diff-targeted sampled clue does not broaden back toward the old higher-mass sampled regime: even after removing raw exposure on unchanged utterances, pushing the sampled-pass count above the live 2-pass center looks stale.

Tested whether the noisy `both_weighted_sampled_diff` clue wants *less* sampled mass than the live 2-pass center by lowering `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes` from 2 to 1 while keeping the original 3:1 nominal weighting, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, this recovered only tie-level life at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.682, but it lost OOD EOS, stretched back to the 53-frame cap, and gt-ASR sanity softened to 0.143 despite in-domain EOS and healthy throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So the diff-targeted sampled clue does not broaden cleanly toward lower sampled mass either: just one deterministic pass softens back to baseline-like life while reintroducing the old long-form OOD failure mode, which makes the original 2-pass setting look even more like the narrow center of this family.

Tested whether the new diff-targeted sampled text mix transfers beyond the plain sampled grouped 2-head path by swapping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` into the older aligned four-head grouped-prefix recipe while keeping nominal normalized/raw weights 3:1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. It recovered only tie-level life at asr_wer_prefix=0.6667 with CER 0.682, EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, and healthy throughput (~30.5k samples / 10 min), but it did not improve on either the reproducible aligned grouped backbone or the first-run 0.5 result from the cleaner 2-head diff-targeted branch. So the diff-targeted sampled clue is not a broad grouped-family upgrade either; it still looks most specific to the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone.

Tested whether the old sampled-branch text anchor can stabilize the new diff-targeted sampled clue by adding `text_loss_weight=0.1` on top of `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff`, nominal normalized/raw weights 3:1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.818 and wildly wrong in-domain transcription despite EOS on both prompts, while collapsing sample coverage to only ~23.6k / 10 min. So the old fragile `text_loss_weight=0.1` spike does not rescue the new diff-targeted grouped clue either; on this branch it behaves like another stale throughput-costly regression rather than a stabilizer.

Tested whether the new diff-targeted sampled clue wants an interior future-depth between the live 2-head grouped backbone and the old 4-head grouped branch by raising `semantic_future_prediction_steps` from 2 to 3 while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff`, nominal normalized/raw weights 3:1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. This recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.955, EOS on both prompts, and reduced coverage (~23.5k samples / 10 min), while both OOD and in-domain transcripts were clearly worse than the live 2-head branch. So the diff-targeted sampled clue does not appear to broaden across intermediate future depth either; it still looks centered on the plain 2-head grouped decoder rather than on a smoother 3-head compromise.

Tested whether the new diff-targeted sampled clue inherits the old shorter-window side path by reducing `semantic_block_decode_max_grouped_tokens` from 12 down to 6 while keeping `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff`, nominal normalized/raw weights 3:1, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the same plain grouped transition. This failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.773 despite EOS on both prompts, decent in-domain WER 0.222, and healthy throughput (~31.2k samples / 10 min). So the diff-targeted sampled clue does not seem to broaden toward the older noisy 6-token side path either; it still looks pinned to the original 12-token grouped window.

Tested a new anchored diff-targeted dataset geometry by adding a temporary `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff_anchor` variant: one full normalized anchor pass plus one diff-targeted weighted sampled pass. I ran it on the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone with `text_source_weighted_normalized_repeats=1`, `text_source_weighted_raw_repeats=1`, and `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, which makes the second pass a 1:1 diff-only sampled perturbation while keeping one guaranteed normalized pass; across the two passes this was meant to approximate an effective 3:1 normalized/raw exposure on the differing subset with less assignment fragility than the current `both_weighted_sampled_diff` winner. After prebuilding the new caches, it failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.818 despite EOS on both prompts, decent in-domain WER 0.222, and full throughput (~31.2k samples / 10 min). So a normalized anchor plus one diff-only sampled perturbation is not the missing stabilizer either: the stronger 0.5 signal from `both_weighted_sampled_diff` does not seem to come from simply anchoring one full normalized pass while preserving an effective 3:1 diff-only exposure on the second pass.

Tested whether the new `both_weighted_sampled_diff` clue is mainly a lucky deterministic sampled assignment by adding a temporary `text_source_weighted_sampled_seed` control and rerunning the plain sampled grouped 2-head diff-targeted recipe at alternate assignment seed `1` while keeping the original 3:1 nominal weighting, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, this softened to only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.727, EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, and full throughput (~31.2k samples / 10 min). So the current 0.5 diff-targeted result does not appear robust across nearby sampled-assignment salts; at least one simple alternate seed already looks materially worse.

Continued the new sampled-assignment sensitivity check for `both_weighted_sampled_diff` by rerunning the same plain sampled grouped 2-head recipe at alternate assignment seed `2` via a temporary `text_source_weighted_sampled_seed` control, while keeping the original 3:1 nominal weighting, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. After prebuilding the new caches, this failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.636 despite EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, decent in-domain WER 0.333, and full throughput (~31.1k samples / 10 min). So the diff-targeted 0.5 clue now looks strongly assignment-sensitive rather than broadly reproducible: one nearby sampled salt softened to 0.8333, and another already collapses all the way back to 1.0.

Tested a new temporary `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff_digit_heavy` variant to see whether the noisy diff-targeted sampled clue improves if the same 2-pass grouped 2-head backbone spends its raw-text budget only on *larger* numeric/date/ID normalization cases rather than on every differing row. The variant kept the same deterministic sampled-pass geometry as `both_weighted_sampled_diff` but only allowed raw text on differing utterances whose raw transcript contains at least four digit characters; with that narrower subset (`838` rows, ~6.4% of the corpus) I used a 1:1 normalized/raw weighting and 2 sampled passes so the overall expected raw exposure stayed close to the live 3:1 diff-targeted recipe. On the plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone (`semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, plain 12-token grouped prefix), this recovered only weak life at asr_wer_prefix=0.8333 with CER 0.591, EOS on both prompts, but degraded gt-ASR sanity to 0.227 and missed both OOD and in-domain transcripts. So the diff-targeted clue is not just coming from concentrating raw exposure on the heaviest numeric verbalization rows; pruning toward only the largest digit-heavy subset looks stale.

Tested a new temporary `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff_spread` variant to see whether the noisy `both_weighted_sampled_diff` clue can be stabilized by changing the *assignment geometry* rather than the mass, ratio, or grouped decoder. This variant kept the same diff-targeted 3:1 raw budget, 2 sampled passes, and plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone, but instead of independently hashing each differing utterance/pass pair it precomputed an exact raw quota and spread raw exposure across as many differing utterances as possible before repeating rows across passes. In other words, it enforced low-overlap, max-coverage raw assignment on the differing subset while leaving unchanged rows normalized. On the live grouped 2-head recipe (`semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, plain 12-token grouped prefix), this failed cleanly at asr_wer_prefix=1.0 with CER 0.727 despite EOS on both prompts, perfect gt-ASR sanity, strong in-domain WER 0.222, and full throughput (~31.4k samples / 10 min). So the diff-targeted 0.5 clue is not rescued by a simple exact-quota / broader-coverage raw assignment either; preserving the same raw budget while explicitly spreading raw exposure across more unique differing rows looks stale.

Tested whether the reproducible fuller-LoRA fallback can stabilize the noisy diff-targeted sampled clue by rerunning the plain grouped 2-head `both_weighted_sampled_diff` recipe with `lora_targets=[q_proj,k_proj,v_proj,o_proj,gate,up,down]` while keeping the original 3:1 nominal weighting, `text_source_weighted_sampled_passes=2`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token grouped prefix. Tested a new grouped-transition variant on the noisy diff-targeted sampled clue by shrinking the plain grouped decoder from 3-token steps down to `semantic_block_decode_size=2` while keeping the same `both_weighted_sampled_diff` 3:1 / 2-pass dataset, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and a 12-token grouped prefix. Tried to probe whether the simpler 2-token grouped transition (`semantic_block_decode_size=2`) broadens beyond the diff-targeted branch by applying it to the reproducible plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, 3:1, 2 passes, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, 12-token grouped prefix). `./autoresearch.sh` crashed before training because this config needed fresh cached `train` / `unpadded` datasets. After prebuilding the required datasets, tested whether the simpler 2-token grouped transition (`semantic_block_decode_size=2`) broadens beyond the diff-targeted branch by applying it to the reproducible plain sampled grouped 2-head backbone (`text_source=both_weighted_sampled`, normalized/raw 3:1, 2 sampled passes, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, p95-style cap, 12-token grouped prefix). Tested a new broader exact-last transfer for the noisy diff-targeted sampled family by combining `text_source=both_weighted_sampled_diff` with the width-matched two-head `semantic_block_decode_size=4` exact/future/future/exact decoder (`semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`) while keeping the original 3:1 nominal weighting, 2 sampled passes, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and a 12-token grouped prefix. Tried to probe whether the width-matching clue transfers back to the aligned normalized grouped backbone by running `text_source=normalized` with `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, and the plain 12-token / 3-token grouped prefix. `./autoresearch.sh` crashed before training because this config needed fresh cached `train` / `unpadded` datasets. After prebuilding the required datasets, tested whether the width-matching clue from the sampled family transfers back to the aligned normalized grouped backbone by running `text_source=normalized` with `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and the plain 12-token / 3-token grouped prefix. Tested whether the width-matched 2-head grouped clue becomes stronger on the aligned normalized backbone when paired with the narrower exact-last grouped transition by running `text_source=normalized`, `semantic_future_prediction_steps=2`, `semantic_block_decode_size=3`, `semantic_block_decode_exact_last_token=true`, future weight 0.5, scheduled sampling 0.5, EOS weight 2.0, the p95-style cap, and a 12-token grouped prefix. Tested whether the failed exec-real wrapper from #635 was bad specifically because it was **symlink-launched**. It still failed cleanly at **`asr_wer_prefix=1.0`** with old-regime throughput (~29.6k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and the logs again reporting `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py` as the task path. So the remaining harm is **not** about the wrapper being symlinked; the act of launching through this outer exec-wrapper surface is itself materially worse than a true copied-script launch.

Tested whether the new **exec-real wrapper** harm from #635/#637 still routes mainly through the `ProcessMixin` child layer. I kept the same direct wrapper launch (not symlinked), still rebinding `__main__`, loader, `sys.argv[0]`, and `co_filename` to `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py` before any benchmark imports, but added **`disable_multiprocessing=true`** on the same split-old-overlays / old-HF / aliased-old-cache surface. That rescued the wrapper surface sharply from `1.0` to **`asr_wer_prefix=0.5`** with old-regime throughput (~29.9k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, short generations (33/37 frames), and clean gt-ASR (`asr_wer_gt=0.0`). So even after canonicalizing the benchmark code itself to the copied-script path, the remaining wrapper/bootstrap harm still routes heavily through the `SyncManager` / monitor-process layer rather than living purely in parent-only state.

Tested whether the new exec-real-wrapper rescue from #638 depends specifically on the `SyncManager`, or on the broader monitor-process layer. I kept the same direct exec-real wrapper surface that canonicalizes `__main__`, loader, `sys.argv[0]`, and `co_filename` to `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py`, but swapped in the old-overlay **no-`SyncManager`** `ProcessMixin` patch instead of using `disable_multiprocessing=true`. This did **not** rescue the wrapper surface: it failed cleanly at **`asr_wer_prefix=1.0`** with old-regime throughput (~29.7k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR. So for the exec-real-wrapper surface, the strong `disable_multiprocessing=true` rescue from #638 is **not** reproduced by removing the manager alone; the remaining harm appears to require the broader monitoring/process layer, not just `SyncManager` creation in isolation.

Tested whether the direct **exec-real wrapper** harm from #637/#639 is specifically coming from the monitor subprocesses rather than the outer wrapper surface or manager alone. I kept the same direct wrapper launch that canonicalizes `__main__`, loader, `sys.argv[0]`, and `co_filename` to `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py`, but swapped in the old-overlay **no-stats** patch so both CPU and GPU monitor subprocesses are disabled while the broader process/manager surface remains. This rescued the wrapper surface from `1.0` to **`asr_wer_prefix=0.6667`** with old-regime throughput (~29.7k samples / 10 min), perfect in-domain prompt match (`asr_wer_prefix_in_domain=0.0`), EOS on both prompts, short generations (27/41 frames), and clean gt-ASR (`asr_wer_gt=0.0`). Combined with #638 and #639, this sharply narrows the wrapper-side culprit: on the exec-real wrapper surface, the main remaining harm is coming from the **stats-monitor subprocess layer**, not from `SyncManager` creation alone.

Tested the **direct exec-real wrapper** surface with the **CPU stats monitor disabled but GPU monitor still active** (old-overlay `/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-nocpu`), keeping the same split-old-overlays / old-HF / aliased-old-cache setup and the same canonical wrapper rebinding to `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py`. This did **not** rescue the wrapper: it failed cleanly at **`asr_wer_prefix=1.0`** with old-regime throughput (~29.6k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and slightly worse gt-ASR (`asr_wer_gt=0.167`). So on the wrapper surface, a remaining **GPU monitor alone is sufficient** to keep the run in the bad class; the no-stats rescue from #640 is not explained solely by removing the CPU monitor.

Completed the wrapper monitor-asymmetry split by testing the **direct exec-real wrapper** surface with the **GPU stats monitor disabled but CPU monitor still active** (old-overlay `/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-nogpu`). This improved over the raw wrapper `1.0` class to **`asr_wer_prefix=0.8333`** with old-regime throughput (~29.5k samples / 10 min), perfect in-domain prompt match, EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR — but it was still clearly worse than the no-stats rescue in #640 and the full `disable_multiprocessing=true` rescue in #638. Together with #641, this sharpens the wrapper-specific culprit: on the exec-real wrapper surface, the **GPU monitor alone is sufficient to keep the run in the bad `1.0` class**, while the **CPU monitor alone is only weakly harmful** (`0.8333`), and removing both monitors recovers the stronger `0.6667` class.

Tested whether the wrapper surface's **GPU-monitor-only** failure from #641 is specifically about a **spawned manager** versus the GPU monitor worker itself. I used the direct exec-real wrapper with the old-overlay **forked manager + CPU disabled** surface (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-forkmgr-nocpu`), so the manager is created under `fork` while the GPU stats monitor remains the only active monitor subprocess. This still failed cleanly at **`asr_wer_prefix=1.0`** with old-regime throughput (~29.6k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and mild gt-ASR drift (`asr_wer_gt=0.1`). So on the exec-real wrapper surface, the harsher **GPU monitor** damage is **not** explained away by the manager being spawned; the GPU-monitor path itself remains sufficient to keep the run in the bad class.

Tested the key wrapper-side hypothesis directly: keep the **direct exec-real wrapper** surface, keep the **GPU monitor as the only active monitor subprocess** (CPU disabled), but force that **GPU monitor worker itself off spawn** by combining the fork-manager + CPU-disabled overlay with the all-fork GPUStats patch. This rescued the wrapper surface from the previous GPU-only `1.0` failures (#641, #643) down to **`asr_wer_prefix=0.6667`** with old-regime throughput (~29.7k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, clean gt-ASR, and much better in-domain prompt match (`asr_wer_prefix_in_domain=0.111`). So the wrapper-side GPU-monitor damage is now shown to be **materially tied to the GPU worker's spawned bootstrap itself**, not just to GPU monitoring logic or manager choice in the abstract.

Isolated how much of the wrapper-side GPU-monitor rescue from #644 comes from **forcing the GPU worker off spawn itself** versus also moving the manager off spawn. I used the direct exec-real wrapper with **CPU disabled**, but this time kept the default spawned manager from the `nocpu` overlay and only replaced `gpu_stats.py` so the **GPU monitor worker** uses `fork`. That improved the raw GPU-only wrapper failure from `1.0` down to **`asr_wer_prefix=0.8333`** with old-regime throughput (~29.7k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR — but it was still clearly worse than #644’s **`0.6667`** when both the manager and the GPU worker were off spawn. So on the exec-real wrapper surface, forcing the GPU worker off spawn is a real rescue by itself, but the strongest recovery still appears to need **both** a non-spawn manager and a non-spawn GPU monitor worker.

Completed the wrapper-side symmetry check for the **CPU monitor path**. I used the direct exec-real wrapper with the old-overlay **forked manager + GPU disabled + CPU worker forced to `fork`** surface (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-forkmgr-forkcpu-nogpu`). This landed at **`asr_wer_prefix=0.8333`** with old-regime throughput (~29.4k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR — essentially no better than the earlier wrapper-side CPU-only spawn case (`#642`). So unlike the harsher GPU path in #644/#645, the wrapper-side **CPU monitor** damage does **not** look strongly tied to its spawn bootstrap; forcing that worker off spawn does not materially improve the wrapper surface.

Added direct **wrapper-side GPU bootstrap instrumentation** by patching the old-overlay `gpu_stats.py` on the **GPU-monitor-only** wrapper surface (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-nocpu-gpudebug`) and reran the canonical direct exec-real-wrapper benchmark. The metric itself was **noisy** — this probe unexpectedly tied the rescued class at **`asr_wer_prefix=0.6667`** with old-regime throughput (~29.9k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR — but the important result is the child dump: the spawned **GPU stats worker** does **not** come up as the wrapper file. It bootstraps with **`argv0=/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py`**, **`main_file=/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py`**, `main_name=__mp_main__`, `main_spec=null`, and `sys.path[0]=/tmp`, with the old overlays immediately after. So unlike the earlier bad symlinked CPU/manager children, the wrapper-side GPU worker already sees the **copied benchmark path** as `__main__`. This materially weakens the idea that the wrapper-side GPU-monitor harm is driven by the same wrong-`__main__` child identity mechanism; the remaining wrapper-specific GPU issue now looks more like a parent/bootstrap or non-`__main__` spawn-surface interaction.

Added the complementary **wrapper-side manager-child bootstrap probe** by reusing the old-overlay `/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-managerdebug-nostats` (manager instrumentation + both stats monitors disabled) under the direct exec-real-wrapper surface. So the remaining wrapper-specific harm is now even less consistent with a simple wrong-`__main__` spawned-child identity story; the live explanation should shift toward the parent/bootstrap surface or some other non-`__main__` spawn-state difference that still couples strongly to the monitor layer.

Ran the complementary **true copied-script control** for the new wrapper-side GPU bootstrap dump: I reused the same GPU-only debug overlay (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-nocpu-gpudebug`) but launched the benchmark directly from `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py` instead of through the exec-real wrapper. Ran the matching **true copied-script control** for the wrapper-side manager-child probe by launching `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py` directly with the old-overlay `/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-managerdebug-nostats` (manager bootstrap dump + both stats monitors disabled). Added a new **parent/child spawn-preparation probe** using a temp `sitecustomize.py` patch (`/tmp/spawnprepdebug/sitecustomize.py`) that dumps `multiprocessing.spawn.get_preparation_data(...)` in the parent and `multiprocessing.spawn.prepare(...)` in the child. Ran the matching **true copied-script control** for the new `multiprocessing.spawn` prep-data probe, again using `/tmp/spawnprepdebug/sitecustomize.py` and the same old-overlay **GPU-only** surface (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-nocpu`), but launching `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py` directly instead of via the exec-real wrapper. Added a deeper **spawn-launch probe** via `/tmp/spawnlaunchdebug/sitecustomize.py`, patching `multiprocessing.popen_spawn_posix.Popen._launch`, `spawn.get_command_line`, `util.spawnv_passfds`, and `reduction.dump` so the parent logs launch metadata for each spawned subprocess. Ran the matching **true copied-script control** for the deeper parent-side launch probe (`/tmp/spawnlaunchdebug/sitecustomize.py`) on the same old-overlay **GPU-only** surface (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-nocpu`). Revisited the **old-train-cache-only** recovery route on the fully local detached old-worktree surface: `/tmp/xax-bench-9cea09c` forced onto `PYTHONPATH`, older HF overlay (`/tmp/xax-overlay-hf452`), and the config/data surface from `/tmp/xaxrc-oldtrain-hf452-local/config.yml` that contains only the old `train/a009c9c8eee13c4a_e4146c08` payload. So the old-train-only path is still alive enough to avoid the clean `1.0` dead class, but it remains unstable and bimodal rather than a reliable recovery: the sequence is now **`0.8333 -> 0.3333 -> 1.0 -> 1.0 -> 0.8333`**.

Built a deeper **manager-child inspect** overlay (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-managerinspect-nostats`) on the bad **symlink-to-copy** current-script surface, starting from the old no-stats / manager-debug patch and extending the spawned manager dump with `inspect.getfile(...)`, loader/package/cached fields, `_path_to_module_name(...)`, resolved-path comparisons, and sample function `co_filename` / `inspect.getmodule(...)` data. On that same old-HF + aliased-old-cache split-overlay surface, the spawn-manager-only diagnostic unexpectedly improved sharply to **`asr_wer_prefix=0.3333`** with old-regime throughput (~30.0k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR. The new child dump matters: even though the spawned manager still comes up with **`main_file=/tmp/current-copy-link.py`** and `inspect.getfile(__main__)` also points at the symlink path, `_path_to_module_name(Path(main_file))` already resolves to **`current_ljspeech_copy`**, `examples.ljspeech` is absent from `sys.modules`, and sampled functions defined in the main module still retain **`co_filename=/tmp/current-copy-link.py`** while imported helpers point into the old overlay modules. Ran the **true copied-script control** for the new deeper manager-child inspect overlay (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-managerinspect-nostats`) on the same old-HF + aliased-old-cache split-overlay **spawn-manager-only** surface. This landed back at **`asr_wer_prefix=1.0`** with the same old-regime throughput (~30.1k samples / 10 min), EOS on both prompts, and clean gt-ASR. The control dump is the important comparison against #656: `_path_to_module_name(Path(main_file))` still resolved to **`current_ljspeech_copy`**, `examples.ljspeech` was still absent from `sys.modules`, `sys.path` matched, and the manager child was still just `__main__/__mp_main__`. The materially different fields were the raw main-script identity itself — `main_file` / `inspect.getfile(__main__)` changed from **`/tmp/current-copy-link.py`** to **`/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py`** — and the sampled main-defined function `weighted_text_sample_bucket` changed its **`co_filename`** in lockstep from the symlink path to the copied path. So the new control strengthens the current explanation: on this symlink-vs-copy manager surface, the remaining live difference is now concentrated even more tightly in the **raw main-module / code-object filename identity**, not in module-name resolution, visible `sys.path`, or visible child import-set differences.

Ablated the new #656 clue on the bad **symlink-to-copy spawn-manager-only** surface by building a minimal manager-child overlay (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-managertouch-nostats`) that does almost nothing beyond touching one main-defined function, `weighted_text_sample_bucket`, and recording its `__code__.co_filename`. This was meant to test whether the #656 rescue was simply caused by accessing a main-function code object in the spawned manager child. It did **not** reproduce: the metric stayed at **`asr_wer_prefix=1.0`** with old-regime throughput (~29.9k samples / 10 min), even though the child dump confirmed the expected symlinked identity (`main_file=/tmp/current-copy-link.py`, `weighted_text_sample_bucket_code_file=/tmp/current-copy-link.py`). So the #656 improvement is **not** explained by merely touching one main-defined function's code filename in the manager child. That prunes the simplest "any code-object touch rescues the bad symlinked manager surface" story; if #656 was mechanistic rather than noise, it depends on a broader introspection/import side effect than this minimal touch.

Exact reran the full **manager-child inspect** overlay from #656 (`/tmp/xaxpkg-oldexact-managerinspect-nostats`) on the same bad **symlink-to-copy spawn-manager-only** surface. That sharply weakens the idea that the full manager-inspect overlay itself is a stable mechanistic repair; in combination with #658, the best reading now is that #656 was either a noise spike or depended on some subtler broad side effect that is not robust enough to chase directly.

Tested whether the lingering **old-train-cache-only** bimodality is materially driven by the same `ProcessMixin` child layer that hurts the mixed current-script surfaces. Tested whether the **old-train-cache-only** detached-worktree route is specifically sensitive to the old `ProcessMixin` **spawn** context rather than just to cache/data provenance. Tried a new **symlink-surface code-object canonicalization** probe by launching a symlinked temp script (`/tmp/current-canoncode-link.py`) whose startup patch rewrites `__main__.__file__` / `argv0` to `/tmp/current_ljspeech_copy.py` and, just before launch, rewrites top-level function / method `co_filename` values to that same copied-script path.
